name: Hoops
historical_reference: Chinese hoop diving; Astley's 42-foot ring, the circus circle itself
card_art: A flaming-edged wooden hoop frozen mid-flight above a row of leaping silhouettes
effect: Base suit. When a Hoops card scores, hoop props sweep the scored row; every talent (skill) card a hoop passes through scores again. Talented spawners suppress their own suit effect.
why_fun: Cards visibly jump through the hoop — the row's composition decides the payoff, so placement is the bet.
balance: Dead in talent-poor rows; a talented card never fires its own hoop; fires only on scoring melds.

name: Knives
historical_reference: Impalement arts — the thrower gets billing, the target girl has the nerve
card_art: A quivering throwing knife buried in a painted board beside an unflinching assistant
effect: Base suit. Knife props launch from the OPPOSITE row edge and score plain (non-skill) prop cards they pass; the spawner scores itself by design.
why_fun: The mirror of Hoops: your chaff finally earns — prop-heavy rows become knife alleys.
balance: Anti-synergy with talent-stuffed rows; rewards keeping cheap cards you would otherwise cut.

name: Balls
historical_reference: Beni Hasan tomb jugglers (~1994 BCE) — the oldest trick on Earth
card_art: Three worn leather balls arcing over an upturned juggler's hat
effect: Base suit. Ball props arc down the column (mancala walk picks talent targets at spawn) and drop the Juggling status: a Juggling card pays out column score when it later scores.
why_fun: Deferred payoff — you seed Juggling now and choreograph the encore next submit.
balance: Payout needs the target to score AGAIN; a cleared board wastes the setup.

name: Flames
historical_reference: Fire eating and full-body burns — the least forgiving skills in the industry
card_art: A gout of orange flame licking up from a performer's raised torch
effect: Base suit. Fire props arc down the column dropping Burning: a Burning card's own suit effect fires with multiplied prop count. Same-act fire cascades (row-scored Burning buffing later columns) are intended.
why_fun: Chain reactions — one good burn turns the whole act into a cascade the player authored.
balance: Multiplies suit COUNT only; worthless on cards that will not score again this act.

name: Gold
historical_reference: Chinese money-suited cards — coins and strings of coins as suits, 1,100 years of precedent
card_art: A heavy coin-stamped card sinking through the deck like a coin in water
effect: Special suit, never rolled randomly. Gold-suit cards ARE currency (full trade value at shops); when one scores, mint a Gold token card (TypeHeavy) to the deck bottom.
why_fun: Scoring literally prints money — the economy build's engine is visible on the table.
balance: Gold tokens bloat and sink the deck; every mint dilutes draws toward dead weight.

name: Electric
historical_reference: Bailey lit the circus by electricity in 1879, before most towns had it
card_art: A crackling blue arc leaping between two metal pylons over sepia canvas
effect: Special suit, never rolled. When an Electric card scores, an arc prop hops card-to-card through orthogonally adjacent cards, +points per link; the chain dies at the first gap.
why_fun: Board topology becomes a circuit — players start wiring the Ring like electricians.
balance: All-or-nothing: one empty slot breaks the chain; wants dense boards, which choke Spotlight.

name: Fireworks
historical_reference: Ruggieri family pyrotechnics — the royal fireworks of 18th-century Europe
card_art: A rocket streaking up a card column leaving a glitter trail
effect: Special suit, never rolled randomly (deck12 is its only grant path). When a Fireworks card scores, the rocket rises its column and banks the column score directly.
why_fun: Instant, loud, column-sized payoff — the crowd-pleaser button.
balance: No status, no chain — pure flat value; weakest in short columns.

name: Roman Numerals
historical_reference: Circus Maximus, Rome — the original circus
card_art: A rank pip carved as marble Roman numerals with laurel trim
effect: Cosmetic rank skin: this card's rank renders as Roman numerals. Melds, stacks and comparisons are completely normal.
why_fun: Cheap collectible shine — rarity borders for people who like marble.
balance: None: pays only in style, competes for a pack slot with real effects.

name: The Blank
historical_reference: Blank stock cards printed in real decks; the sideshow blow-off mystery tent
card_art: A card that is only an ivory rectangle with a faint question of a shadow
effect: No rank, no suit. Stacks on anything; anything stacks on it; never melds and scores 0. Pure spatial glue.
pseudocode:
class PipRankBlank extends PipRank:
  func on_compare_ranks(a, b): return 0.0    # always stackable
  func on_compare_suits(a, b): return NAN    # incomparable: never a flush
  # excluded from melds by returning no rank value to Scoring
why_fun: Teaches the deepest lesson — position is worth points you cannot see.
balance: A permanently dead scoring slot; every Blank lowers your ceiling.

name: The Joey
historical_reference: Joseph Grimaldi — clowns are still called Joeys after him
card_art: A Jack in whiteface and motley juggling his own crown
effect: Face rename: Jacks are Joeys. A Joey counts as a Clown-class card for group effects.
pseudocode:
class PipRankJoey extends PipRankNumeral:  # rank 11
  func get_class_tag() -> StringName: return &'Clown'
  # group lookups (leaders, Greasepaint) read get_class_tag from pips too
why_fun: Faces stop being dead royalty and join the troupe — Jack synergy decks appear.
balance: Tag only helps if you run Clown payoffs; otherwise an ordinary Jack.

name: The Ringmistress
historical_reference: Mollie Bailey and Agnes Lake — women who owned and ran American circuses
card_art: A Queen in a scarlet tailcoat cracking a whip of ribbon
effect: Face rename: Queens are Ringmistresses. Counts as a Producer-class card for group effects.
pseudocode:
class PipRankRingmistress extends PipRankNumeral:  # rank 12
  func get_class_tag() -> StringName: return &'Producer'
why_fun: Same trick as the Joey — the court becomes staff you can build around.
balance: Tag-only value; needs class payoffs to beat a plain Queen.

name: The Impresario
historical_reference: James A. Bailey — the logistics genius behind Barnum's name
card_art: A King buried in route maps, one gloved hand raising a train whistle
effect: Face rename: Kings are Impresarios. Counts as a Producer-class card and gives +1 trade value when spent at shops.
pseudocode:
class PipRankImpresario extends PipRankNumeral:  # rank 13
  func get_class_tag() -> StringName: return &'Producer'
  func trade_value() -> int: return 1 + base_trade_value()  # shop layer reads this
why_fun: Kings become walking gift certificates — spending face cards finally feels right.
balance: The +1 only matters if you sell him; in the Ring he is a vanilla King.

name: The Headliner
historical_reference: Top billing on circus posters; the real solitaire ace rule
card_art: An Ace as a spotlit performer alone on the poster, name in lights
effect: Face rename: Aces are Headliners. A Headliner counts HIGH or LOW in runs (1 or 14), decided at evaluation time in your favor.
why_fun: Answers the notes' own question — yes, Aces are the actual performers, and duality is a real spatial decision.
balance: Flexibility, not power: still one card, and greedy high/low reads can wreck a planned run.

name: Half-Step
historical_reference: Sideshow in-between acts; HalfStepRank already stubbed in the code
card_art: A pip painted as five and a half stars, the half star winking
effect: Rank N½: stacks legally between N and N+1 in runs but never participates in poker melds.
why_fun: Glue for impossible runs — the fixer card that smooths a broken column.
balance: Its column run gets longer but the poker axis loses a card: a real toll.

name: The Fifteen
historical_reference: Cribbage — invented by Sir John Suckling, 17th-century poet and card sharp
card_art: A pip of two dice faces summing fifteen inside a brass ring
effect: This card ALSO melds with any adjacent neighbors whose ranks sum to 15 with it (the demoted cribbage rule — an ability, not a default).
why_fun: Resurrects the vetoed cribbage scoring exactly as the veto asked — as a rare ability you hunt sums for.
balance: Arithmetic tax: you warp placements chasing 15s that a flush would have paid better.

name: The Best Bower
historical_reference: The Joker — born as Euchre's Best Bower in 1860s America, the deck's newest card
card_art: A grinning jester pip peeking from behind a fanned deck
effect: This card counts as EVERY suit for flush evaluation (never for suit-stacking rules).
why_fun: The flush enabler every suit build wants — and an open Balatro homage.
balance: Spawns no props (no suit effect at all); pure meld grease.

name: The Half Card
historical_reference: Johnny Eck, 'The Amazing Half-Boy' — performer, artist, sawing-gag legend
card_art: Literally the top half of a card, cleanly cut, standing proud
effect: Half rank (rounds down), half size: TWO Half Cards occupy one board slot together and meld as one combined card.
why_fun: Two acts in one slot — board compression as a collectible.
balance: Alone it is a weak half-rank; the payoff needs the pair drawn and placed together.

name: Harlequin
historical_reference: Arlecchino's diamond motley — the visual ancestor of harlequin card backs
card_art: A pip diamond-patterned in two suit colors at once
effect: Dual-suit pip: this card counts as BOTH printed suits for every comparison, meld, and suit effect (both props fire).
why_fun: One card, two flushes, two prop volleys — the placement puzzle squared.
balance: Cannot stack on either of its suits (both count); double props double the chaos you must plan for.

name: The Fire-Knife
historical_reference: Samoan siva afi — the ailao war dance, machete wrapped in burning towels
card_art: A spinning machete wreathed in flame trails against night
effect: Fixed dual-suit card: Knives AND Flames simultaneously — knife props fly the row while Burning drops down the column.
why_fun: The one-card argument for dual suits: a whole act in a single scored card.
balance: Feeds both prop economies at half focus; suit-hate towns punish it twice.

name: The Wildcard
historical_reference: The user's wild pip: tracks the previous pip inside itself, rerolled from the board
card_art: A pip of shifting stained glass, mid-transformation
effect: Wild pip: on every board change it becomes a copy of a rank/suit currently present in its row, chosen deterministically. Cycling visuals show the current identity.
why_fun: A shapeshifter you can herd — move its neighbors and you choose what it becomes.
balance: You never fully control it; a bad row makes it a bad card, and it re-rolls when you least want.

name: The Forged Ace
historical_reference: The English Ace of Spades duty stamp — forging it hanged Richard Harding in 1805
card_art: An ornate Ace of Spades a little TOO ornate, ink still wet
effect: Event-granted counterfeit: enormous rank (counts as two Aces in melds). If any inspection effect or Prepared Town examines it, it is destroyed and this show's fame payout is voided.
why_fun: History's highest-stakes single card — every inspection event becomes a heart attack.
balance: One inspection doesn't just kill the card, it hangs the whole show's payout.

name: Paper
historical_reference: The printed playing card itself — cheap stock democratized the deck
card_art: A plain card stock texture with a faint watermark
effect: Default material. No modifier behavior; the baseline every other type upgrades.
why_fun: The floor everything else is measured against.
balance: It is the absence of an effect — strictly outclassed by any real material.

name: Canvas
historical_reference: The big top itself — canvas bosses and roustabouts raised it town after town
card_art: A tight weave of tent canvas with a stitched patch
effect: Sturdy material: the first debuff/status applied to this card each show is discarded (canvas shrugs it off).
why_fun: Quiet insurance that makes hazard towns feel beatable.
balance: Only the FIRST hit; sustained status pressure goes straight through.

name: Ticket Stub
historical_reference: 'Annie Oakley' — circus slang for a punched free ticket, after her card-shooting act
card_art: A punched pasteboard ticket, one hole shot clean through
effect: Currency chit: worth double trade value at shops, scores 0 and never melds in the Ring.
why_fun: Small denominations for the cards-as-currency economy — pocket change that feels like loot.
balance: Dead weight on the board; every stub drawn is a mulligan you did not get.

name: Sawdust
historical_reference: The sawdust ring — Astley's original 42-foot horse ring floor
card_art: A soft pile of golden sawdust holding the print of a vanished card
effect: When this card is discarded from the Ring, its board slot keeps a sawdust marker: the next card placed there gains +points on its first score.
why_fun: Makes discarding SPATIAL — you salt the ground where the next act will stand.
balance: Pay now, collect later, and only if you actually replant that exact slot.

name: Heavy
historical_reference: Robert-Houdin's Light and Heavy Chest — weight as a trick
card_art: A card sagging with iron rivets along its border
effect: Material: after any shuffle or return to deck, this card sinks to the deck bottom. Gold token cards use this.
pseudocode:
# IMPLEMENTED: TypeHeavy
class TypeHeavy extends CardModifierType:
  func on_append() -> void:  # entering a deck
    game.defer(func(): game.sink_to_bottom(data))
why_fun: Predictable gravity — you always know where the gold is.
balance: Bottom of the deck is the last card you will ever draw; heavy value is slow value.

name: Helium
historical_reference: The Light material's named form (adopted from the §14.5 summary list)
card_art: A card straining upward against its own string like a parade balloon
effect: Material: after any shuffle or return to deck this card rises to the top; during Next it is always the first card drawn.
why_fun: Guarantees your engine piece shows up NOW — tempo you can print on a card.
balance: Always drawn first means always exposed first — hazards and discards find it too.

name: Air
historical_reference: Early materials list: Earth, Air, Water, Fire with custom movement
card_art: A card of pale cloud, edges wisping away
effect: Elemental material: whenever its stack settles, this card floats to the TOP of its stack.
why_fun: A card that insists on the spotlight — self-sorting stacks feel alive.
balance: It hogs the topmost (active) slot, smothering whatever skill you WANTED spotlit.

name: Earth
historical_reference: Early materials list: Earth, Air, Water, Fire with custom movement
card_art: A card of packed loam and root, heavier at the base
effect: Elemental material: whenever its stack settles, this card sinks to the BOTTOM of its stack, carrying its stamp with it.
why_fun: Foundation cards that dig in — build the column ON it and it never gets in the way.
balance: Buried means covered: without Revealing its skill is dark all game.

name: Water
historical_reference: Early materials list; Vietnamese water puppetry — performers hidden waist-deep
card_art: A card of rippling water, reflections of its neighbors on its face
effect: Elemental material: on Next, if an adjacent column is shorter, this card flows sideways into it (always seeking the lowest level).
why_fun: Self-leveling boards — it patches your thin columns while you look elsewhere.
balance: It WILL wander out of the meld you were building; water does not take direction.

name: Tinder
historical_reference: Early materials list; the fire triangle — fuel, heat, spark
card_art: A card of dry kindling and pitch, one corner already charring
effect: Elemental material: whenever this card is covered by another card, the covering card catches Burning.
pseudocode:
class TypeTinder extends CardModifierType:
  func on_card_dropped_on(dropped) -> void:  # fires on automated drops
    var st : StatusBurning = StatusBurning.new()  # duplicate at application
    game.defer(func(): dropped.add_status(st))
why_fun: A trap material — you PLANT it and let the game stack fuel on it.
balance: Burning multiplies suit count only on cards that score again; random coverage wastes it.

name: Stone
historical_reference: TypeStone stub in code; the notes' 'cards that can resist being moved'
card_art: A card of chiselled granite, moss in the pip grooves
effect: Material: once placed in the Ring this card cannot be grabbed again (blacklist wins). It scores +bonus for standing firm.
why_fun: Commitment as a mechanic — placing it is a promise the board must keep.
balance: A misplaced Stone is a permanent wall in your own Ring.

name: Rosin
historical_reference: Rosinback horses — dusted with rosin so bareback riders stick
card_art: A card dusted white, a bootprint stuck fast to its face
effect: Sticky material: cards placed onto this card cannot be picked up again, but score +1 performance ring deeper than they stand.
why_fun: Commitment for reward — you glue your best act down on purpose.
balance: Everything it holds is held FOREVER this show; rescue is impossible.

name: Glass
historical_reference: Magic-lantern slides; the notes' own 'kind of like a glass card'
card_art: A translucent card, light refracting through a hairline crack
effect: Material: double points when scored. Shatters (destroyed) permanently after its third score.
why_fun: Borrowed brilliance with a countdown — every score is louder because it is numbered.
balance: You are burning the candle: three shows of glory, then a hole in the deck.

name: Wax
historical_reference: Barnum's American Museum waxworks — burned down, twice
card_art: A card slowly slumping, one pip drooping like a candle
effect: Material: at each show end, lose 1 rank for every Flames-suit card that shared its column this show. Melts to nothing at rank 0.
why_fun: Environmental storytelling — the deck itself remembers who it stood next to.
balance: Free value in fireless decks; a liability the moment you draft Flames.

name: Flash
historical_reference: Juggling slang: a 'flash' — all props thrown once, nothing sustained
card_art: A card mid-camera-flash, image overexposed white
effect: Material: the FIRST time this card would score each show it scores triple; every later score that show is zero.
why_fun: Choreography skill: WHEN a card enters scoring becomes the whole decision.
balance: Multi-scoring engines (Hoops, Encore, Double Trigger) actively waste it.

name: Digital
historical_reference: The notes' 'Digital card, cannot discard from board'
card_art: A card of glowing scanlines that flickers when touched
effect: Material: this card cannot be discarded from the board by any effect — hazards, discards, and board clears leave it in place.
why_fun: An anchor the weather cannot blow away — Blowdown towns hate it.
balance: It also refuses YOUR discards; you cannot cycle it when it clogs a column.

name: Null
historical_reference: The notes' null cards — space-takers, highlighted when selected
card_art: An empty card frame around bare table felt
effect: Junk material inserted by hazards: takes up a slot, scores nothing, melds nothing. Highlighted when selected so hover reads clearly.
why_fun: The obstacle that makes clean boards feel earned.
balance: Pure downside by design — the question is how fast you can bury or dump it.

name: First Act
historical_reference: Playbill structure — the opener performs to a settling crowd
card_art: A card stepping alone through parted curtains, house lights still on
effect: Deck-position type: after every shuffle this card moves to the top of the deck and enters play alone, before any other card that Next.
why_fun: Guaranteed openers — your engine piece is always on stage by turn one.
balance: An opener drawn alone is also an opener unprotected; it eats the first hazard.

name: Second Act
historical_reference: Playbill structure — the turn arrives when the crowd is warm
card_art: A card waiting in half-shadow at stage left, one foot in the light
effect: Deck-position type: after every shuffle this card is placed exactly at the deck's midpoint and enters play alone.
why_fun: Mid-show reinforcements you can set your watch by.
balance: Midpoint timing is rigid: too late for a fast rush, too early for a finale.

name: Final Act
historical_reference: Top billing closes the show — the headliner walks last
card_art: A card silhouetted against a full-house standing ovation
effect: Deck-position type: after every shuffle this card sinks below all non-Heavy cards and enters play only after the rest of the deck is drawn. Scores +mult as the closer.
why_fun: The finale you plan three submits around — delayed gratification with billing.
balance: If the show ends before the deck empties, your closer never performed.

name: Lithograph
historical_reference: Strobridge litho posters — for most towns the ad WAS the show
card_art: A card that is itself a gorgeous circus poster, corners pasted down
effect: Poster material: while this card sits in the Grand Entrance (upper zone, not the Ring), its skill is active globally. In the Ring it is just paper.
pseudocode:
class TypeLithograph extends CardModifierType:
  func is_active_override() -> Variant:  # consulted by CardModifier.is_active
    if zone_of(data) == UPPER: return true
    if zone_of(data) == LOWER: return false
    return null
why_fun: A card that wants to be NOT played — it inverts the game's whole incentive.
balance: It occupies an Entrance slot forever, slowing every draw behind it.

name: Tintype
historical_reference: Tintype photography — the fair's cheap souvenir portraits
card_art: A silvery photographic plate bearing another card's faded image
effect: Photograph material: on acquisition, permanently copies the pips (rank + suit) of the card it was created from. Chains of photos of photos are legal.
pseudocode:
class TypeTintype extends CardModifierType:
  func on_acquired(source : CardData) -> void:  # booster/shop layer
    data.rank = source.rank.duplicate()
    data.suit = source.suit.duplicate()
    data.meta.lineage = source.meta.lineage + 1
why_fun: Collectors chase photo-of-a-photo lineages like provenance.
balance: It copies pips only — no skill, no stamp; a portrait, not the performer.

name: Automaton
historical_reference: Karakuri ningyō — the Edo tea-serving doll; Maskelyne's whist-playing Psycho
card_art: A card with visible brass clockwork under a porcelain face
effect: Material: every Next this card performs its cue automatically for free — but it can NEVER be cued manually.
why_fun: Set-and-forget clockwork — you build a machine and watch it run.
balance: No timing control: it fires on ITS schedule, wasting cues the turn you needed them held.

name: Bahurupiya
historical_reference: Bahurupiya — 'of many forms', arriving each day as a different god or official
card_art: A card mid-costume-change, four different collars visible at once
effect: Material: at the start of each show this card arrives as a random different class (deterministic per seed), counting for that class's group effects.
why_fun: A different guest star every show — the yak-shave engine in card form.
balance: You cannot build AROUND it; it supports whatever it feels like being today.

name: Outlawed
historical_reference: The skomorokhi — Russia's minstrel-clowns, banned by church and tsar in 1648
card_art: A card behind a nailed-up town proclamation, eyes defiant
effect: Material: in pious/lawful towns this card cannot score at all; everywhere else it scores DOUBLE. Underground shows find a way.
why_fun: Route planning becomes deck planning — you tour AROUND the bans.
balance: Map dependence: a lawful stretch of road turns it into cargo.

name: Spangle
historical_reference: Sequined costumes — 'sawdust and spangles', the classic circus memoir title
card_art: A single oversized sequin stitched to the card's corner, catching light
effect: Stamp: +1 to the act's combo (U) the first time this card is part of any scored meld each act.
why_fun: Simple combo glue every build wants — the common-tier workhorse.
balance: One U per act, no points of its own; pure grease.

name: Marked
historical_reference: The card sharp's quiet arts — marked decks, shiners, readers
card_art: A tiny scuffed thumbnail notch only you would notice on the card back
effect: Stamp: you always see where this card sits in the draw deck (its position is shown on the deck viewer and above the deck slot).
why_fun: Quiet, permanent information — every Next becomes a countable card.
balance: Information only; it never adds a point, and cheat-hate towns may fine it.

name: Brass Check
historical_reference: Brass checks — token coinage of circus and carnival midways (the 'money on trigger' seal, themed)
card_art: A stamped brass token half-sunk into the card face
effect: Stamp: whenever this card's effects trigger, mint a Gold token card onto the top of the draw deck.
why_fun: Turns any trigger-happy card into a mint — economy from choreography.
balance: Gold on deck TOP delays real draws; overminting clogs your tempo.

name: Greasepaint
historical_reference: Clown white — the Auguste and whiteface makeup traditions
card_art: A smear of white greasepaint across the card's portrait
effect: Stamp: this card counts as a Clown-class card in addition to its own class.
why_fun: The class-splashing enabler every tribal system needs — anyone can join the troupe.
balance: A tag is only as good as your Clown payoffs; blank without them.

name: Top Billing
historical_reference: Poster billing wars — stars fought over letter size on the lithographs
card_art: The card's name printed enormous across a poster masthead
effect: Stamp: this card always scores FIRST within its meld (trigger-order control, before board-order dispatch).
why_fun: Makes the invisible trigger-order system player-facing — sequencing becomes a build.
balance: Order only matters with order-dependent effects; otherwise dead ink.

name: Ghost Light
historical_reference: The theatre ghost light left burning on empty stages (Ghost Card, adopted as a stamp)
card_art: A bare bulb on a stand glowing through a translucent card
effect: Stamp: this card does not block the Spotlight of cards beneath it — they stay active as if uncovered.
why_fun: Lets players build TALL without strangling their own engines — the counterweight the ring-depth tension needs.
balance: The ghost-lit card itself still needs its own activation; stacking two is redundant.

name: Revealing
historical_reference: The eye seal — 'trigger even when hidden'; mini skill-art drawn inside the eye
card_art: An open eye stamp with a miniature of the card's own skill art as its iris
effect: Stamp: this card's modifiers stay active while covered (spotlight not required).
why_fun: Freedom to bury your engine and keep building over it.
balance: Board-only: it does nothing from decks, and the card still cannot be grabbed if buried illegally.

name: Maximum Effort
historical_reference: The Exhaust stamp note — 'kind of like a glass card'
card_art: A performer mid-leap, veins of gold light, sweat flying
effect: Stamp: double points from this card on score; afterwards it is Exhausted (scores nothing) until a show passes in which it was never played.
why_fun: A card you must deliberately REST — roster management like a real troupe.
balance: Its off-shows are real: half your tour it is a blank.

name: Understudy
historical_reference: Theatre understudies — ready in the wings, word-perfect
card_art: A shadowed figure in the wings mouthing the star's lines
effect: Stamp: if the card directly above this card in a stack is destroyed or consumed, this card permanently copies that card's skill.
why_fun: Turns destruction effects (Gluttony, Glass) into setup plays — grief into inheritance.
balance: Needs precise stacking under a card that will actually die; a plan with two failure points.

name: Marotte
historical_reference: The jester's scepter topped with a miniature of his own head
card_art: A tiny grinning copy of the card's own face on a stick, tucked in its belt
effect: Stamp: when this card is destroyed, a rank-1 copy of it (same skill, no stamp) survives in its place.
why_fun: Death becomes a discount, not an ending — insurance with a punchline.
balance: The copy is rank 1 and stampless; you saved the act, not the star.

name: Iron Body
historical_reference: Shaolin hard qigong — spear against throat, iron-head demonstrations
card_art: A card flexing, a snapped spear haft falling away from its chest
effect: Stamp: immune to the first destruction or debuff that targets this card each show.
why_fun: Walking into a hazard town with armor on changes how brave your placements get.
balance: One block per show; the second knife lands.

name: Ascetic
historical_reference: Bed-of-nails and firewalking endurance traditions across cultures
card_art: A serene card lying unbothered on a bed of tiny nails
effect: Stamp: this card is immune to row- and floor-targeting hazards (lava rows, flooded rows, burning columns pass under it harmlessly).
why_fun: Hazard boards become solvable puzzles — one card walks the coals.
balance: Only environmental hazards; a targeted boss effect ignores the nails.

name: Unpierceable
historical_reference: Rifa'i dervish demonstrations — blades through flesh without harm
card_art: A knife passing through the card leaving no mark, both halves whole
effect: Stamp: Knife props pass through this card harmlessly — and still count as a 'miss' for stuntman/target bonuses.
why_fun: Makes knife rows farmable from BOTH sides — miss bonuses without the wound.
balance: Knife-specific; every other suit's props treat it as paper.

name: The Vine
historical_reference: Naghol land diving, Pentecost Island — the vine-ankle ritual that inspired bungee
card_art: A green liana knotted around the card's ankle-corner
effect: Stamp: the first time this card would be destroyed or discarded by a fall/hazard each tour, it stops a hair short and survives.
why_fun: The near-miss made literal — the crowd gasps, the card dangles, the run continues.
balance: Once per tour, hazards only; a plain player discard still goes through.

name: Contract Ink
historical_reference: Circus performer contracts — stars jumping between rival shows
card_art: A signature line on the card still wet, pen resting across it
effect: Stamp: +X points per show in which this card is played; if benched two shows in a row, it leaves the troupe (removed from the deck).
why_fun: Anti-autopilot pressure — a card that demands attention or walks.
balance: It punishes exactly the flexibility big decks rely on.

name: Double Trigger
historical_reference: The original Seals list — trigger twice
card_art: Two overlapping wax seals, slightly offset like a double exposure
effect: Stamp: all of this card's effects trigger twice (once per card per scoring pass).
why_fun: The purest amplifier — every skill in the catalog gets a second life.
balance: Doubles duds too; worthless on cards with passive or once-per-show effects.

name: Global
historical_reference: Descendant of the 'trigger when in deck' seal
card_art: A seal shaped like a little globe, meridians glowing
effect: Stamp: this card's modifiers are active from ANYWHERE — board, Entrance, draw deck, discard.
why_fun: Deck-resident engines — the card works while it waits.
balance: Global also means always exposed to 'all active cards' hazards; no off switch.

name: The Encore
historical_reference: Audiences demanding encores; Leitzel's one-arm planges counted aloud by the crowd
card_art: A performer bowing amid thrown roses, one hand raised for quiet
effect: Stamp: if this card's skill triggered this show, 50% chance (seeded) it triggers once more at show end.
why_fun: The end-of-round lottery tick — Balatro's Lucky-card dopamine slot, diegetic.
balance: A coin flip on an effect you already used; variance, not floor.

name: Lloyd's Policy
historical_reference: Performers insured body parts with Lloyd's of London — Leitzel's arms, dancers' legs
card_art: An elegant insurance certificate stamped with a red underwriter's seal
effect: Stamp: the first time this card would be destroyed or debuffed, prevent it and PAY OUT: mint 2 Gold tokens.
why_fun: Insurance that pays like insurance — being attacked becomes a small win.
balance: One claim per policy; after payout the card is bare.

name: Kuroko
historical_reference: Kabuki stagehands the audience agrees not to see
card_art: A figure in perfect black against black, only the card's pips visible
effect: Stamp: this card is unseen — it does not block Spotlight beneath it AND cannot be targeted by boss/town effects.
why_fun: The Ghost Light's rare upgrade — invisible scaffolding for tall builds.
balance: Untargetable includes YOUR targeted buffs; kindness cannot find it either.

name: Bauta
historical_reference: The Venice Carnival mask that let all classes mingle unreadably
card_art: A white beaked mask over the card's face, black tricorn above
effect: Stamp: this card's rarity and identity are hidden — inspections, towns, and effects treat it as unknown until it scores.
pseudocode:
class StampBauta extends CardModifierStamp:
  func is_inspectable() -> bool: return false
  func apparent_rarity() -> int: return RARITY_UNKNOWN
  func on_score(result) -> void: reveal(data)  # scoring unmasks for the act
why_fun: Smuggling a legendary through a Prepared Town behind a blank white mask.
balance: Hidden identity blanks positive identity-keyed synergies too.

name: Self-Granted Title
historical_reference: 'Lord' George Sanger — who ennobled himself to out-bill Buffalo Bill
card_art: A tin coronet drawn onto the card's portrait in gold marker
effect: Stamp: this card counts as one rarity higher for all effects and bonuses (but never for pack/pool rolls).
why_fun: A commoner in a paper crown outperforming its price tag — the bluff pays real dividends.
balance: Rarity-keyed payoffs only; without them the crown is tin.

name: The Illustrated
historical_reference: Betty Broadbent — 565 tattoos, forty years touring
card_art: A card whose every inch of skin is dense with inked scenes
effect: Stamp: this card may hold unlimited stamps — but can never hold a skill.
why_fun: A living scrapbook — the collection build's centerpiece card.
balance: All support, no act: it amplifies a deck but headlines nothing.

name: The Avatar
historical_reference: VTubers — kayfabe reborn as a streaming industry
card_art: A luminous cartoon mask hovering an inch in front of the card's face
effect: Stamp: this card wears another deck card's face — it copies that card's name/class/identity for all checks until unmasked (scored 3 times).
why_fun: Identity as a costume — the unmasking on the third score is a fanfare moment.
balance: The persona's HATERS apply to you too, and the reveal is on a timer you cannot stop.

name: Ringmaster's Grip
historical_reference: The Ringmaster leader spec — the stamp that can always be picked up
card_art: A white-gloved hand closing firmly around the card's edge
effect: Stamp: this card (and the stack it heads) can ALWAYS be picked up, regardless of stack legality.
why_fun: Freedom of movement in a game about frozen stacks — the hand is always yours.
balance: Grabbing was half the rule; placing the illegal stack somewhere is still your problem.

name: The Wedding Band
historical_reference: Tom Thumb's 1863 wedding — the media event of its year; Lincoln received the couple
card_art: Two tiny gold rings tied to the card by a white ribbon
effect: Stamp (applied to two cards): the pair always shuffles adjacent and is always drawn together.
why_fun: Combo pieces that refuse to be separated — consistency as romance.
balance: Two deck slots married forever; if one is dead weight, so is the date.

name: The Belt
historical_reference: The championship belt — held, defended, taken
card_art: A gaudy gold championship belt strapped around the card's middle
effect: Stamp: exactly ONE card in your save file holds the Belt. It scores double. Belt-challenge bosses can TAKE it — win it back at the next meeting.
why_fun: A title with lineage across your whole save — losing it writes a story arc.
balance: It can literally be taken from you, and the champion draws every challenger.

name: Input Column
historical_reference: The 'even input slots are cards' principle
card_art: A trapdoor in the Grand Entrance floor, card-shaped light below
effect: Rule card (one per column): on Next, drops its upper stack into the paired Ring column, then draws a card from the deck to refill.
why_fun: The heartbeat rule — every Next is this card doing its job.
balance: Engine default; removing one shrinks your whole economy of draws.

name: Ring Column
historical_reference: Rule-deck layout cards — duplicate rules identified by rank
card_art: A numbered wooden stake marking one ring column's ground
effect: Rule card (one per column): adds one Ring (lower zone) column at game creation. Rank = which column (zone adder #1 is rank 1).
why_fun: Board width as cards — bosses can steal a column by stealing a card.
balance: Losing one mid-run breaks every 5-wide poker assumption you built on.

name: OG Grabber
historical_reference: Klondike's tableau rules — the ancestral solitaire grab law
card_art: A hand lifting a fanned run of cards, alternating glow on their edges
effect: Rule card: default grab legality — a stack may be picked up if it is an ascending or descending run with no two same-suit cards adjacent.
why_fun: The base law every movement card gets to break — rules exist to sell exceptions.
balance: As written it locks most mid-game boards; that friction IS the game.

name: OG Placer
historical_reference: Klondike's tableau rules — the ancestral placement law
card_art: A card clicking into place atop a column like a drawer closing
effect: Rule card: default placement legality — a stack may be placed where the join continues a legal run without same-suit contact.
why_fun: The other half of the base law — placement tension on every drop.
balance: Same as the Grabber: the wall your movement cards are priced against.

name: Cascade Scorer
historical_reference: Whole-board evaluation — the loop v2 decision
card_art: An usher's lamp sweeping down the rows of a darkened board
effect: Rule card: on Submit, walks the lower zone scoring all rows first, then all columns simultaneously per row band.
why_fun: The whole board matters — the rule that makes Solatro not-Balatro.
balance: Whole-board scoring means whole-board mistakes; nothing is a sideboard.

name: Poker Evaluator
historical_reference: Standard poker hand rankings, row-wise
card_art: A magnifying glass over five cards, the best hand glowing inside the lens
effect: Rule card: evaluates each scored line for its best poker hand; only the first/best scored poker hand per line pays.
why_fun: Poker literacy transfers in — everyone already knows what a full house is.
balance: Best-only scoring makes second hands in a row worthless; density has a cap.

name: Free Undo
historical_reference: The notes: '1 free undo per turn, baked in by default'
card_art: An hourglass with one grain of sand hovering above the waist
effect: Rule card: one free undo per turn that does not carry over. (Snapshot undo exists engine-side; this card is the per-turn allowance.)
why_fun: Permission to experiment — the take-back that keeps new players moving.
balance: One per turn: the second mistake is still yours.

name: Label
historical_reference: The 'Baba Is You nonsense' note — an Evident card announces its hidden rule
card_art: A luggage tag tied to nothing, plainly describing an invisible law
effect: Rule card: while in the rules deck, its OWN effect text is announced on the board (visible to the player) even though the rules deck stays hidden.
why_fun: The first crack of light into the hidden rule deck — reading the law becomes content.
balance: Announcing a rule does not let you change it — yet.

name: Soundcheck
historical_reference: Roadies and the empty-hall soundcheck before doors open
card_art: A single card on a bare stage, one work light, cables coiled
effect: Rule card: at show start you get one free rehearsal move — grab and place one stack before the first Next, no act consumed.
why_fun: Opening-hand agency — you shape the board before the show starts.
balance: One move against a whole opening layout; sequencing skill still rules.

name: Audience Patience
historical_reference: The notes' patience system — moving cards drops patience; at zero, cards drop themselves
card_art: A pocket watch face where the numerals are tiny yawning faces
effect: Rule card: the audience has N patience per turn; each grab/place spends 1; unique effect triggers refund 1; at 0 patience the Entrance drops on its own.
why_fun: Every fidget costs — actions get weight, and showmanship literally buys time.
balance: Punishes exactly the rearranging that solitaire fingers itch to do.

name: The Booking
historical_reference: Evel Knievel's declared jumps; fame wagering from the tour notes
card_art: A signed contract nailed to a post: a score, a town, a date
effect: Rule card (contract): declares this show's goal and terms (e.g. 'score X with no Knife melds'). Fulfill it for bonus fame; miss it and the failure is worse.
why_fun: Failure becomes SPECIFIC — 'missed by 40' panic, manufactured every show.
balance: You are volunteering for constraints; greed writes checks the deck must cash.

name: Brass Ring
historical_reference: Carousel brass rings — grab one for a free ride
card_art: A brass ring on an iron arm extended over one column of the board
effect: Rule card: a brass ring token rotates one column per turn; if that column scores while ringed, the meld pays double and you bank a Ticket Stub.
why_fun: A moving jackpot the whole table orbits — timing becomes tangible.
balance: Chasing the ring warps play; sometimes the free ride costs the show.

name: Performance Rings
historical_reference: The rings note matured from 'every 5 rows make row red'
card_art: Concentric painted rings on the board floor, deeper rings glowing hotter
effect: Rule card: score zones by board depth — rows 1–5 pay 1x, rows 6–10 pay 2x, rows 11–15 pay 3x, and so on.
why_fun: Depth pays — and deep columns cover your own spotlights: the game's central tension, priced.
balance: Tall play risks burying every skill you own; the multiplier taxes your activation economy.

name: Symmetrical Show
historical_reference: User-authored: 'ability where poker hands are also considered vertically'
card_art: A mirrored theatre — the same hand of cards reflected as row and column
effect: Rule card: poker hands are ALSO evaluated on columns (runs stay column-native; hands score both axes).
why_fun: Doubles the puzzle space in one sentence — every column becomes a second hand.
balance: Column hands fight column runs for the same five slots; you cannot have both.

name: The Contrary
historical_reference: Sacred-clown contrariness (concept only, generic name per the care list)
card_art: A rider facing backwards on a forward-galloping horse
effect: Rule card: while in play, descending runs count as ascending, and the WORST poker hand each row scores instead of the best.
why_fun: Junk decks become royalty for one show — the whole value system somersaults.
balance: Your curated deck is suddenly the worst one in the room.

name: Kayfabe
historical_reference: Pro wrestling's never-broken fiction — protected like a trade secret for a century
card_art: A referee winking at the audience while the folding chair swings
effect: Rule card: while in play, all Gaff/Humbug/fake cards COUNT AS REAL — they score as what they pretend to be. Remove it and everything reverts.
pseudocode:
class RuleKayfabe extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_active() -> void: game.state.kayfabe = true
  func on_deactive() -> void: game.state.kayfabe = false
  # Gaff/Humbug on_score checks state.kayfabe before revealing/deflating
why_fun: The fiction as infrastructure — an entire fake deck becomes championship-real.
balance: Your whole payroll is a lie with a single load-bearing card.

name: Sword Swallower
historical_reference: The sword-swallowing column — one perfect descending run, deck to floor
card_art: A column of cards swallowed straight down a performer's raised gullet
effect: Secret meld (hidden rule): a single column that is one perfect descending run from board top to floor pays a massive bonus and banks a Knife-suit card.
why_fun: Discovering it feels like finding a real trick — and then you build for it on purpose.
balance: A full perfect column is the least flexible formation the board allows.

name: Dead Man's Hand
historical_reference: Wild Bill Hickok — aces and eights, Deadwood, 1876
card_art: Two pair face-up on a saloon table, one chair knocked over
effect: Secret meld (hidden rule): score two pair, Aces over Eights, in one row — morbid jackpot plus a ghost event on the map.
why_fun: A jackpot you half-dread assembling — the table goes quiet when the eights land.
balance: Chasing exact two pairs means declining better hands on purpose.

name: The Forty-Two
historical_reference: Astley's ring is 42 feet because of horse physics — hide 42 everywhere
card_art: The number 42 chalked faintly on the ring floor under the sawdust
effect: Secret meld (hidden rule): when a row score plus its column score totals exactly 42, the act's combo gains +3 U.
why_fun: Numerology the community will reverse-engineer — the wiki page writes itself.
balance: Steering exact totals in a multiplier game is gloriously impractical.

name: The Three Rings
historical_reference: The three-ring circus — Barnum and Bailey's split-focus invention
card_art: Three complete card rows glowing as three circus rings at once
effect: Secret meld (hidden rule): score three separate 5-card poker hands (three full rows) in ONE submit → permanently unlock a third-zone rule card for your profile.
why_fun: A discovery that permanently changes YOUR copy of the game — the deepest unlock in the design.
balance: Three simultaneous full hands demands a board most decks cannot even hold.

name: The Voladores
historical_reference: Danza de los Voladores — four flyers, thirteen circles each: 4 x 13 = 52
card_art: Four cards descending on ropes from a pole, circling a fifth
effect: Secret meld (hidden rule): in one show, score four different columns each containing a 13-rank descent → the deck-as-calendar bonus: bank one full year (52x base) of fame.
why_fun: The cosmic solitaire secret — the 52-card deck revealed as a calendar, once a career.
balance: Functionally a lifetime achievement; it will pay out for one player in ten thousand.

name: The Yakuza Hand
historical_reference: Oicho-kabu: 8-9-3 (ya-ku-za), the worthless hand that named the yakuza
card_art: Three cards — an 8, a 9, a 3 — fanned defiantly on bare ground
effect: Secret meld (hidden rule): score a meld of exactly 8-9-3 → the worthless-hand jackpot pays out massively.
why_fun: The game rewarding the WORST hand is the best joke a scoring system can tell.
balance: 8-9-3 is a garbage meld every other rule punishes you for holding.

name: The Butterfly Trick
historical_reference: Ukare no chō — paper butterflies kept alive with a fan, Japan's classic conjuring piece
card_art: Two paper butterflies rising from an open fan over the board
effect: Cue: create two rank-1 paper-token cards (any suit) that drift down one chosen column, one row per Next.
why_fun: Living confetti — free bodies for runs and Kecak counts, drifting where you fanned them.
balance: Rank-1 filler can pad a run but dilutes every poker row it lands in.

name: Mystery Box
historical_reference: Sideshow blow-off tents — pay first, see after
card_art: A crate stamped with question marks, lid straining
effect: On game start, this card becomes a random skill for the rest of the run (deterministic per seed).
why_fun: The cheapest lottery ticket in the game — sometimes the box has a tiger.
balance: You budgeted a deck slot for literally anything, including a dud.

name: The Egg Bag
historical_reference: Isaac Fawkes' egg bag, fairground magic since the 1720s
card_art: A velvet bag turned inside out, provably empty, obviously not
effect: Cue while spotlit: stash the top discard inside this card. When this card scores, the stashed card pops into the Grand Entrance.
why_fun: Recycling the discard pile through a velvet bag — the reveal always lands.
balance: Two-step tempo: stash now, cash only when the bag itself scores.

name: Sawing in Half
historical_reference: P.T. Selbit, 1921 — the golden-age illusion
card_art: A card halfway through a glittering box, both halves waving
effect: Cue: split a target card into two cards of half rank (one rounded up, one down), same suit; this card exhausts for the show.
why_fun: Rank arithmetic as a saw — fabricate the exact run link you were missing.
balance: You destroyed a big card to make two small ones; poker rows hate the surgery.

name: Misdirection
historical_reference: Sleight-of-hand fundamentals — the audience watches the wrong hand
card_art: A gloved hand pointing left while the right palms a card
effect: While spotlit: the topmost covered card in each adjacent column is also treated as spotlit.
why_fun: One placement re-lights the board — a spatial aura you steer by walking.
balance: The aura moves when it moves; adjacency is a leash on your best column.

name: The Digesting Duck
historical_reference: Vaucanson's 1739 mechanical duck — the digestion was faked with hidden pellets
card_art: A brass duck swallowing a card, the card visibly sliding into a drawer below
effect: Consumes cards dropped on it like a Hippo — but secretly stores them; cue to cough any one back into the Entrance.
why_fun: Fake Gluttony with a refund button — eat problems now, spit answers later.
balance: Storage is not scoring: cards in the duck do nothing until you burp them out.

name: Pandora's Box
historical_reference: Pandora's box — hope stays inside
card_art: An ornate box exhaling a different-colored smoke each dawn
effect: On game start becomes a random skill; on game end resets to Pandora's Box (the roll never sticks).
why_fun: A fresh gamble every single show — the box never runs out of surprises.
balance: You can never keep the good roll; the box always closes.

name: Metamorphosis
historical_reference: Houdini and Bess — the substitution trunk, three seconds flat
card_art: Two trunks mid-swap, a handcuffed silhouette in each
effect: Cue: swap this card with any card in the draw deck (seen or unseen), taking its board position.
pseudocode:
class SkillMetamorphosis extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var pick : CardData = cue_pick_from_deck()  # unseen unless revealed
    game.defer(func():
      game.swap_board_and_deck(data, pick)
      game.register_combo(combo_key()))
why_fun: A controlled gamble with a body — trade yourself for the unknown.
balance: Unseen swaps are honest coin flips; the trunk does not care what you hoped.

name: The Ambitious Card
historical_reference: Dai Vernon's Ambitious Card fooled Houdini three times in 1922
card_art: A card rising to the top of a squared deck, one corner smugly bent
effect: This card always rises to the top of the draw deck after any shuffle or discard; +points when drawn the same show it was discarded.
why_fun: It keeps coming back — the deck itself performs the trick at you.
balance: Always on top means always first into whatever the town does to your draws.

name: The Card Index
historical_reference: Arthur Lloyd, 'the Human Card Index' — any card produced from his pockets on request
card_art: A coat flung open, hundreds of indexed pockets fanned with cards
effect: Cue (once per show): search the draw deck for ANY card and put it into the Grand Entrance.
why_fun: The tutor effect, historically literal — consistency in one pocket.
balance: One fetch per show, and fetching is not placing: the combo still has to survive the board.

name: Walking Through the Wall
historical_reference: P.T. Selbit walked through a brick wall in 1914
card_art: A card halfway through a brick wall, bricks politely parting
effect: Cue: move this card THROUGH an occupied slot to the empty slot beyond it (passes through one card).
why_fun: Ignores the one law solitaire holds sacred — that cards block cards.
balance: One-slot range; the wall must have an empty far side.

name: Pepper's Ghost
historical_reference: The 1862 angled-glass illusion — still how dead musicians tour
card_art: A translucent duplicate of a card shimmering at 45 degrees to its original
effect: While spotlit: an intangible duplicate of this card appears in the OPPOSITE zone; it scores in melds as if real but cannot be touched, moved, or targeted.
why_fun: Scoring in two zones at once with one card — the mirror trick as math.
balance: The ghost dies the moment the original is covered; fragile double-dipping.

name: Psycho
historical_reference: Maskelyne's whist-playing automaton at Egyptian Hall
card_art: A turbaned clockwork figure laying a card with a soft tick
effect: Automaton skill: every Next, plays the top card of the draw deck into this card's column automatically.
why_fun: A second dealer at the table — the board grows while you think.
balance: It plays WHATEVER is on top, including the card you were saving from exactly this.

name: Dedi's Mercy
historical_reference: Dedi of the Westcar Papyrus — reattached the severed heads of geese
card_art: A robed figure rejoining a torn card, seam glowing gold
effect: Once per show: when one of your cards is destroyed, restore it to the discard pile instead (the head reattaches).
why_fun: Consumption engines get a safety net — feed the tiger with a clear conscience.
balance: Saves to DISCARD, not to hand; and only the first death matters.

name: The Turk
historical_reference: Von Kempelen's 1770 chess automaton — a hidden human inside; Poe helped expose it
card_art: A chessboard automaton whose cabinet door hangs one inch open
effect: Automaton whose power is the secret beneath: its rank counts as the rank of the card directly under it in the stack. Remove that card and the Turk stops (rank 0).
why_fun: A card powered by its own hidden operator — the stack IS the trick.
balance: Two slots to run one act, and exposing the bottom card kills the show.

name: The Imitator
historical_reference: Chung Ling Soo vs. Ching Ling Foo — the imitator out-billed the original
card_art: Two identical posters pasted overlapping, the top one slightly gaudier
effect: Choose a deck card at acquisition: this card is a Gaff copy of it, scoring 150% of the original's base — but only while the original is NOT on the board.
why_fun: Running the knockoff act while the original tours elsewhere — deliciously shameless.
balance: Draw both together and the imitator deflates on the spot.

name: The Calm Voice
historical_reference: Harry Blackstone Sr. evacuated a burning theater by announcing it as an illusion
card_art: A magician mid-bow gesturing the audience calmly toward glowing exits
effect: Once per run: when a disaster event or boss effect fires, negate it and convert it into a show — gain fame equal to its severity.
why_fun: The catastrophe becomes the encore — the coolest save in the catalog.
balance: One use per run, and you must HAVE him on board when the fire starts.

name: Bian Lian
historical_reference: Sichuan face-changing — masks swapped in an instant, method guarded within families
card_art: A performer mid-turn, half the face one mask, half already another
effect: Cue: instantly swap this card's rank and suit with any adjacent card's. The game never shows how (no animation, no log entry).
pseudocode:
class SkillBianLian extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var n : CardData = cue_adjacent_target()
    game.defer(func():
      var r = data.rank; data.rank = n.rank; n.rank = r
      var s = data.suit; data.suit = n.suit; n.suit = s
      game.register_combo(combo_key()))
why_fun: Pip surgery at cue speed — and the concealment is the flavor.
balance: Adjacent only: the mask reaches exactly one card in each direction.

name: Frankenstein
historical_reference: Shelley's creature — assembled from parts, stronger than any donor
card_art: Three cards stitched into one tall body with visible thread
effect: On submit, merges with the card above and below it into one 3-card merged stack (summed rank) that stays in the zone after scoring.
why_fun: A monster that outlives the board wipe — you build next show around what it became.
balance: The donors are gone forever; a bad stitch makes a big dumb card.

name: The Light and Heavy Chest
historical_reference: Robert-Houdin's electromagnet chest — a child lifts it, a strongman cannot
card_art: A small brass-bound chest, one handle gleaming, one handle groaning
effect: This card's weight flips by mover: YOU can always grab it (light), but board effects and hazards cannot move or discard it (heavy).
why_fun: The trick is WHO is lifting — mover-dependent physics is a delightful rules joke.
balance: It protects itself, not its column; and it never scores extra.

name: Utter Chaos
historical_reference: The chaos clause of the Mystery Box chain
card_art: A skill card whose art is repainting itself mid-frame
effect: Becomes a random skill on EVERY Next (deterministic per history hash).
why_fun: A slot machine that pulls its own lever every turn — pure variable-ratio candy.
balance: You cannot plan one turn ahead; chaos giveth mid-combo and taketh mid-combo.

name: Grand Vanish
historical_reference: Copperfield vanished the Statue of Liberty, 1983
card_art: An entire card column missing, its silhouette outlined in stage light
effect: Cue (once per show): hide an entire column — it cannot be seen, targeted, scored, or touched for one turn, then returns with +points on every card ('with interest').
why_fun: Yanking a whole column out of a boss's reach is the biggest dodge in the game.
balance: A vanished column also skips YOUR scoring pass; timing it wrong vanishes your act.

name: 72 Transformations
historical_reference: Sun Wukong, the Monkey King — Peking opera's trickster acrobat
card_art: A staff-wielding monkey mid-shift, seventy-one faint afterimages behind
effect: Wild skill: may transform (copy any spotlit card's skill) on cue; the game counts transformations across the run — at 72, this card ascends (permanently upgrades to Legendary stats).
pseudocode:
class Skill72 extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    data.skill = copy_of(cue_target().skill); GameData.relink_card_backrefs(data)
    run_state().wukong_count += 1
    if run_state().wukong_count >= 72: ascend(data)  # profile-permanent
why_fun: A 72-step collection quest living on one card — the ascension is a career goal.
balance: Each transformation overwrites the last; the count wants variety your run may not offer.

name: The Bullet Catch
historical_reference: The trick that killed Chung Ling Soo, 1918 — magic's most fatal illusion
card_art: A card catching a musket ball between painted teeth, smoke still crossing the stage
effect: When scored in the Spotlight: score 10x rank. 1-in-13 chance (seeded) it is destroyed instead.
why_fun: The purest near-miss thrill in the design — players will tell stories about the night it fired.
balance: Twelve gorgeous catches mean nothing to the thirteenth; it WILL eventually die mid-run.

name: Tumbler's Poise
historical_reference: The charge-system exemplar from the notes — one charge, cue or auto-spend
card_art: A tumbler frozen in a handstand on one finger, chalk dust hanging
effect: 1 charge per turn: cue to hop this card one column sideways; an unused charge auto-spends on Next (it tumbles on its own).
why_fun: The charge system's tutorial card — use it or watch it use itself.
balance: Auto-spend can tumble it OUT of the meld you parked it in.

name: The Acrobat
historical_reference: Adopted from the §14.5 summary list — grab-while-covered
card_art: A lithe figure sliding out from under a stack of heavier cards
effect: This card can be grabbed even while covered by other cards (the cards above cascade down one slot).
why_fun: Rescuing a buried card is the solitaire fantasy — this card rescues itself.
balance: It escapes alone; whatever was stacked above collapses into your problem.

name: Diabolo
historical_reference: Kongzhu, the Chinese yo-yo — centuries old, strung between two sticks
card_art: A spinning diabolo arcing between two card columns on a taut string
effect: Every Next this card alternates between two adjacent columns, granting +points to the top card of each column it leaves.
why_fun: A card that commutes — its schedule becomes part of your board clock.
balance: Its column is never stable; runs that include it dissolve every other turn.

name: The Leotard
historical_reference: Jules Léotard — invented the flying trapeze AND the garment
card_art: A card in a one-piece mid-flight between two swinging bars
effect: Cue: move this card and everything above it to the top of any column — legality ignored (the flying trapeze does not ask permission).
why_fun: Stack teleportation — it breaks placement law the way trapeze breaks gravity.
balance: It carries its whole stack, wanted or not; the landing column eats everything.

name: Colleano's Somersault
historical_reference: Con Colleano — first feet-to-feet forward somersault on the tightwire
card_art: A wire-walker flipping forward over his own shadow
effect: Cue: this card flips OVER the card covering it — they swap positions (the move considered impossible).
why_fun: The buried card that un-buries itself — one flip and the spotlight is yours again.
balance: One card of cover only; a deep burial is still a burial.

name: The Wing Walker
historical_reference: Lillian Boyer and Ormer Locklear — barnstorming's wing-to-wing transfers
card_art: A figure stepping between two biplanes made of falling cards
effect: During the Next-phase drop, this card may transfer mid-air from its input column to an adjacent one (choose on cue before Next).
why_fun: Mid-air rerouting — the drop phase stops being fate and starts being flying.
balance: You commit before the draw; the plane you jump to may be the wrong one.

name: Hopak Burst
historical_reference: Hopak — the Ukrainian squat-kick dance, explosive and competitive
card_art: A dancer mid-kick launching a whole stack of cards upward
effect: Cue (1 charge/show): jump this card's entire stack one row upward in its column (everything beneath slides up).
pseudocode:
class SkillHopak extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.charges.get(data, 0) <= 0: return
    game.state.charges[data] -= 1
    game.defer(func(): game.shift_stack_rows(data, -1))
why_fun: Row math on demand — one kick and the Performance Ring boundary moves for you.
balance: One burst per show; the row above must exist to land in.

name: The Dzhigit's Pickup
historical_reference: Cossack dzhigitovka — snatching objects off the ground at full gallop
card_art: A rider hanging sideways off a galloping horse, fingertips brushing a card
effect: During Next, snatch ONE falling card as it drops and redirect it to any column (chosen live).
why_fun: Catching the draw mid-fall — the single most cinematic interrupt in the game.
balance: One catch, chosen blind: you commit to catching before you see what falls.

name: Mallakhamb Pole
historical_reference: Mallakhamb — gymnastics wrapped around the wrestler's pole
card_art: A polished wooden pole with performers spiraling up its length
effect: While spotlit: every card in this card's column gains +points per row of column height when the column scores.
why_fun: Makes TALL the strategy — the pole pays you for the game's riskiest shape.
balance: Tall columns bury spotlights; the pole taxes its own activation.

name: The Stilt-Walker
historical_reference: Moko jumbies and stilted guardians (concept borrowed generically per the care list)
card_art: A card on tall striped stilts, head and shoulders above its stack
effect: This card is spotlit even when covered by ONE card (it sees over the head in front).
why_fun: Half a Revealing stamp on a skill slot — tall builds get one free floor.
balance: The second card of cover still blinds it.

name: The Handicap
historical_reference: Maria Spelterini crossed Niagara with peach baskets strapped to her feet
card_art: A wire-walker in peach baskets, the falls roaring beneath
effect: Cue at show start: voluntarily lock this card's movement for the show (cannot be grabbed); it scores DOUBLE all show.
why_fun: Betting on your own placement — declare the crossing, then live with the wire.
balance: Doubled and nailed down: if the board shifts, your star cannot.

name: Flagpole Sitter
historical_reference: Shipwreck Kelly — weeks perched on poles above city crowds
card_art: A tiny figure atop a flagpole made of one very tall card column
effect: Gains +points for each consecutive show it ends as the TOP card of the same column; any move resets the streak.
why_fun: A multi-show commitment you can SEE growing — the pole gets taller in your head.
balance: A column you can never rebuild; the streak dies to one good reshuffle.

name: Human Cannonball
historical_reference: Zazel (1877), then the Zacchini dynasty — the launched performer
card_art: A figure blasting out of a cannon, trailing a card-shaped smoke ring
effect: Cue: fire the BOTTOM card of any column to the top of any other column.
why_fun: Reaching the UNREACHABLE card — the bottom of the stack is the solitaire fantasy.
balance: The shot card arrives on TOP, covering whatever engine was working there.

name: Blondin's Crossing
historical_reference: Charles Blondin crossed Niagara on a rope — once carrying his manager
card_art: A tightrope strung between two board columns, a whole row inching across
effect: Once per show: shift an ENTIRE row one column left or right, wrapping around the board edge.
pseudocode:
class SkillBlondin extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.blondin_used: return
    game.state.blondin_used = true
    game.defer(func(): game.rotate_row(cue_row(), cue_direction()))  # Board-level op
why_fun: The biggest single spatial verb in the game — the whole board takes one step.
balance: Every column's run breaks at once; you fix five problems or cause five.

name: The High Dive
historical_reference: Steel Pier's high-diving horses; Sonora Webster kept diving blind
card_art: A card plunging from the board's top edge toward a tiny tank
effect: Cue: drop this card from the top of its column to the bottom; score +points per row fallen.
pseudocode:
class SkillHighDive extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var fall : int = rows_below(data)
    game.defer(func():
      Board.move_stack(game.state, data, Board.ColumnStart.new(1, col_of(data)))
      game.add_line_score(col_of(data), DIVE_PTS * fall)
      game.register_combo(combo_key()))
why_fun: Height converts to points on impact — you build the tower just to jump off it.
balance: It lands at the BOTTOM: buried, dark, and done for the show.

name: The Clock Hang
historical_reference: Harold Lloyd dangling from the clock face in Safety Last! (1923)
card_art: A card hanging off the board's last column by one bent corner
effect: May be placed half-off the board past the last column: while hanging it is double-spotlit (skill fires twice) — but any board-shift effect drops it (discarded).
why_fun: Living on the ledge — double output while every gust in the design can kill you.
balance: One Blondin, one hazard, one wobble: the street is a long way down.

name: The Backward-Backward
historical_reference: May Wirth — the only performer to land the blind backward-backward, horse to horse
card_art: A rider somersaulting backward off one galloping card onto another
effect: Cue: reverse the run direction of one column for the rest of the show (descending counts as ascending and vice versa).
why_fun: Mid-run direction changes rescue columns everyone else writes off.
balance: One column only, and every FUTURE placement there must obey the flipped law.

name: The Contortionist
historical_reference: Mongolian uran nugaralt — the world's great contortion lineage
card_art: A performer folded impossibly inside a single card-width outline
effect: May fold to SHARE one board slot with another card (two cards, one position; both count in melds).
why_fun: A genuinely new spatial verb — six cards in a five-card row.
balance: Six-card 'hands' confuse best-hand evaluation; the fold can dilute a flush.

name: The Traceur
historical_reference: Parkour and the Yamakasi — the city as apparatus
card_art: A runner vaulting across the tops of five card columns without touching down
effect: This card moves only across the TOPS of columns (never enters stacks): cue to run any distance sideways along topmost slots.
why_fun: The board as terrain, not slots — freedom of the rooftops.
balance: Always topmost means always first into hoop, knife, and hazard alike.

name: The Crowd Surf
historical_reference: Stage diving — trust the crowd or eat the floor
card_art: A card passed hand-to-hand across a sea of upturned card faces
effect: Cue: pass this card across the tops of all columns to the far edge, +points per column crossed; if ANY crossed column is empty it drops (discarded).
why_fun: A risk meter drawn in real board state — you can SEE the gap before you dive.
balance: One empty column anywhere on the route is a funeral.

name: The Pyramid
historical_reference: The Moroccan tumbling brotherhoods of Sidi Ahmed ou Moussa — 'Arab tumblers' on every golden-age bill
card_art: Six figures in a 3-2-1 human pyramid spanning three columns
effect: Formation meld: when three adjacent columns hold stacks of exactly 3, 2, and 1 cards, the formation scores as a bonus meld (+U).
why_fun: Board silhouettes become scoring objects — you sculpt the skyline itself.
balance: Three columns held hostage to a shape that one draw ruins.

name: The Triple
historical_reference: The salto mortale — the triple somersault that killed trapezists until Codona mastered it
card_art: Three blurred rotations above a net that looks very far away
effect: If this card completes a run of EXACTLY 3 that was fully assembled this turn, that run scores triple.
why_fun: Rewards CONSTRUCTING the trick this turn, not owning it — turn-scoped skill expression.
balance: Exactly three, all placed this turn: hesitation or greed both void the bonus.

name: The Courier
historical_reference: Andrew Ducrow's 'Courier of St. Petersburg' — standing astride multiple horses
card_art: A rider standing astride two galloping card columns, reins in each hand
effect: While spotlit, this card straddles TWO adjacent columns — it counts as a member of both columns' melds.
why_fun: The single best spatial legendary available — one body, two acts.
balance: Two columns of exposure, and covering him breaks both saddles at once.

name: Flash Mob
historical_reference: Sudden coordinated performance in public space — assembly as the trick
card_art: Fifty cards mid-leap from the Entrance onto the board at once
effect: Cue (once per show): deploy your ENTIRE Grand Entrance onto the board in one action (columns chosen left-to-right automatically).
pseudocode:
class SkillFlashMob extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.flashmob_used: return
    game.state.flashmob_used = true
    game.defer(func():
      for c : CardData in game.state.upper_cards():
        Board.move_stack(game.state, c, Board.ColumnEnd.new(1, auto_col(c))))
why_fun: The whole crowd hits the floor at once — tempo as spectacle.
balance: Automatic placement: the mob lands where it lands, melds be damned.

name: The Seven Pyramid
historical_reference: The Flying Wallendas' seven-person pyramid — collapsed in 1962; the family rebuilt and went on
card_art: Seven cards in a trembling three-tier pyramid, wire beneath
effect: Formation: a single stack of exactly 7 legally-run cards scores a colossal bonus. If the stack is ever broken by a hazard, rebuild it once for double.
why_fun: The most famous formation in circus history — and the rebuild clause honors why.
balance: Seven legal cards in one tower is the whole show balanced on one column.

name: The Puszta Five
historical_reference: The csikós of Hungary — standing astride five galloping horses
card_art: A rider standing on five horses abreast, each horse a card column
effect: Cue (once per show): for one scoring pass this card counts as a member of EVERY column's meld (the Courier's endgame evolution).
why_fun: One card scoring in five melds at once — the board folds around a single rider.
balance: One pass per show, and he must survive uncovered until you call it.

name: Klyaksa
historical_reference: Karandash and his dog Klyaksa ('Inkblot') — the USSR's defining clown duo
card_art: A small ink-black dog token trotting beside a clown card
effect: Comes with a free rank-1 Dog token card (Animal) that shuffles into your deck and follows this card's column when drawn.
why_fun: A pet that follows you around the board — pure charm with a body count of zero.
balance: Two deck slots for one act, and the dog is a rank-1 body.

name: Flea Circus
historical_reference: Victorian flea circuses — real fleas, real harnesses
card_art: A magnifying glass revealing a tiny big top on a card's back
effect: When scored, counts as FIVE cards of rank 1 for combo-counting purposes (U and class counts, not points).
why_fun: Tiny card, huge combo — the joke IS the mechanic.
balance: Zero extra points: it inflates the multiplier and nothing else.

name: The Monkey
historical_reference: Sarumawashi — Japanese street monkey performances, a thousand years old
card_art: A capuchin in a tiny vest climbing a column of cards
effect: Spawns a monkey token that climbs its column one row per Next, cueing whatever card it sits on (free cue).
why_fun: A free finger on the cue button, wandering upward — you build the ladder it climbs.
balance: It cues what it LANDS on, including the card you were saving.

name: Liberty Horses
historical_reference: 'Liberty' acts — riderless horses forming figures on voice command alone
card_art: White horses wheeling in formation, no riders, one raised whip
effect: On Next, all Animal-class cards in the Grand Entrance rearrange themselves into ascending rank order before dropping.
why_fun: Auto-sorting is deeply satisfying — the herd dresses its own line.
balance: Only Animals, only the Entrance: the Ring's chaos is still yours.

name: Hungry Hippo
historical_reference: The hippo card from the original TODO list — implemented then gutted
card_art: A cavernous pink yawn beneath two tiny greedy eyes
effect: Consumes cards dropped onto it, adding their rank to its own (cap 13 total); returns everything eaten at show end.
why_fun: Feeding the hippo is toddler-grade joy with real board consequences.
balance: Everything fed is off the board till show end; cap 13 arrives fast.

name: The Learned Pig
historical_reference: Toby the Sapient Pig — spelled, read minds, told time; Johnson and Byron both remarked
card_art: A bespectacled pig tapping a card with one trotter
effect: Once per turn: reveals the description/identity of any facedown, hidden, or unknown card (Gaffs stay smug but their text shows).
pseudocode:
class SkillLearnedPig extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.pig_used: return
    game.state.pig_used = true
    if game.view: game.view.reveal_text(cue_target())  # info only, data untouched
why_fun: The counter-intelligence pet — Gaff economies stop working on you.
balance: Pure information: the pig never adds a point.

name: Clever Hans
historical_reference: Clever Hans, the counting horse — actually reading his questioner's body language
card_art: A horse tapping a hoof, eyes locked sideways on its neighbor
effect: Appears to calculate: counts as a copy of its highest adjacent neighbor's rank. If it ever has no neighbors, it is revealed as rank 1.
why_fun: A fraud that works exactly as long as the audience stands close — placement as kayfabe.
balance: Isolate him once and the whole act collapses to a 1.

name: The Gentled Beast
historical_reference: Carl Hagenbeck's reward-based training — kindness made the cage obsolete
card_art: A great cat leaning into an open palm, no bars anywhere
effect: Alternative to eating: cards dropped on this card are not consumed but BEFRIENDED (returned to the column below it); each friend permanently +1 this card's score bonus.
why_fun: The ethical fork of the eating mechanic — kindness is mechanically superior, on purpose.
balance: Slower than Gluttony and the friends still occupy board slots.

name: The Lion Dance
historical_reference: Chinese lion dance — two performers inside one lion, plucking the greens
card_art: A festival lion rearing, two pairs of shoes visible beneath the fringe
effect: Secretly TWO cards: when destroyed, it splits into two rank-half performers instead of dying. Placing it on a card 'plucks the greens': harvest +points from the card beneath.
why_fun: The kill shot that becomes two performers taking a bow — the reveal never gets old.
balance: The halves are half-rank bodies; surviving is not the same as thriving.

name: Mabel's Tigers
historical_reference: Mabel Stark — working big cats bare-handed into her seventies
card_art: A woman in white leathers, one glove resting on a tiger's head
effect: Consumes cards dropped on it like Gluttony — but ONLY prop (skill-less) cards; +1 rank per meal, no cap, permanent.
why_fun: A curated Gluttony that eats your chaff and respects your talent — deck thinning with teeth.
balance: It only ever grows off junk; a lean deck starves the cats.

name: The Unicorn
historical_reference: Menagerie 'unicorns' — a one-horned rhino billed as legend
card_art: A rhinoceros in profile, horn gilded, poster overhead screaming UNICORN
effect: Animal Gaff: scores as a Legendary (5x base) until any inspection effect examines it — then it is revealed (rhino) and scores half forever.
why_fun: Riding the bluff show after show — every Prepared Town is a held breath.
balance: One inspection converts your headliner into a permanent rhino.

name: The Lion's Bride
historical_reference: The menagerie's caged-beauty tableaus — beauty seated among the beasts
card_art: A serene figure seated in a cage, a lion's head heavy in her lap
effect: Scores bonus points every act it shares a column with a consuming Animal card (Tigers, Gluttony, Hippo) without being eaten.
why_fun: Points for proximity to your own monsters — the cage door is the thrill.
balance: One careless drop and your bride is hippo food.

name: The Ox
historical_reference: Bumba Meu Boi — the pageant ox killed and resurrected amid celebration
card_art: A garlanded ox mid-collapse, confetti already falling
effect: This card's destruction IS its act: when destroyed, the town celebrates (+fame) and it revives into the deck next show.
why_fun: Dying on schedule for applause — destruction decks finally get a renewable body.
balance: It WANTS to die: alive on the board it is a vanilla ox.

name: Jumbo
historical_reference: Barnum's Jumbo — the elephant so famous he became the word for big
card_art: An elephant filling the whole card frame, a top hat tiny on his head
effect: Heavy. Cards cannot be placed on Jumbo; Jumbo can be placed on anything; +points per card beneath him when scored.
