why_fun: A column-capper with WEIGHT — you end a column with him on purpose, forever.
balance: He seals his column shut; whatever is under him is under him for good.

name: Sin of Gluttony
historical_reference: The seven deadly sins by way of the hippo card 'on crack' (the notes' own words)
card_art: A vast gilded maw ringed by seven candles, deck cards spiraling in
effect: No cap, feeds from ANYWHERE (board, Entrance, decks — cue to feed), permanently consumes; rank grows without limit.
why_fun: Unbounded number-go-up powered by cannibalizing your own deck — the purest devil's bargain in the catalog.
balance: Every point of rank is a card that no longer exists; the deck you win with may not survive the winning.

name: HONK
historical_reference: The bulb horn — clowning's one-note orchestra
card_art: A red rubber bulb horn, mid-squeeze, sound lines everywhere
effect: On cue: makes a honk noise. Unlimited uses. That is the entire card.
why_fun: The tap-mechanic tutorializer that asks nothing and delivers a honk every time.
balance: Occupies a deck slot and a board slot to produce one (1) noise.

name: Slapstick
historical_reference: The battacio of commedia dell'arte — the literal slap-stick
card_art: Two wooden slats mid-clap across a startled card's face
effect: Cue: swap the RANKS of this card and one adjacent card.
why_fun: Cheap, tactile, always-useful pip fixing — the whack is the fix.
balance: One swap, adjacent only; it moves numbers, never bodies.

name: The Kecak Chorus
historical_reference: Kecak — a hundred interlocking voices as one orchestra, Bali, 1930s
card_art: Dozens of tiny rank-1 cards in concentric rings, arms raised, chanting
effect: When any meld scores, +mult per rank-1 card currently on the board (the chorus chants for every act).
why_fun: Makes an army of worthless 1s into an instrument — mass-commons proudly viable.
balance: A board full of 1s is a board that melds almost nothing on its own.

name: Custard Pie
historical_reference: Silent-film and circus pie fights — aim is everything
card_art: A custard pie frozen an inch from a dignified face
effect: Cue (throw): target card's suit becomes Balls; if that completes a flush row, +mult immediately.
why_fun: Suit-fixing that feels like AIMING — the splat completes the flush.
balance: One pie, one suit, one direction: Balls or nothing.

name: Tappable Clown
historical_reference: Adopted from the §14.5 summary list — the 3-tap randomizer
card_art: A wind-up clown with a halo of three segmented lights, one dimmed
effect: 3 charges per show (halo shows remaining): cue to deterministically re-roll the ranks of all adjacent cards.
pseudocode:
class SkillTapClown extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_game_start() -> void: game.state.charges[data] = 3
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.charges[data] <= 0: return
    game.state.charges[data] -= 1
    for n : CardData in adjacent_cards(data):
      n.rank = hash_rank(game.state.game_history_trimmed, n)  # no RNG
why_fun: A pocket slot machine with a visible ammo counter — the halo begs to be spent.
balance: Re-rolls hit EVERY neighbor, including the one that was already perfect.

name: The Sunshine Clown
historical_reference: Oleg Popov — the gentle wordless clown beloved across the Cold War divide
card_art: A clown in a checked cap holding a sunbeam like a balloon string
effect: While spotlit: removes one debuff/status per turn from the cards directly below it. Only buffs, never gags.
why_fun: A healer in a game of hazards — the kind clown makes debuff towns humane.
balance: Heals downward only, one per turn; he cannot save himself.

name: Mr. Punch
historical_reference: Pulcinella became England's Mr. Punch — 'That's the way to do it!'
card_art: A hook-nosed puppet mid-swing, slapstick connecting with a neighbor
effect: Each act, gains points for every adjacent card that gets debuffed or destroyed (gleefully amoral).
why_fun: Profiting from the neighborhood's misfortune — the audience laughs anyway.
balance: He needs suffering adjacent to him; a healthy board starves the puppet.

name: The Jongleur
historical_reference: Medieval jongleurs — jugglers, tumblers, singers, news-carriers, all at once
card_art: A patched figure juggling a lute, three balls, and a rumor
effect: Counts as EVERY class for group effects — but all its class bonuses apply at half value.
why_fun: The universal splash card — every leader smiles at him a little.
balance: Master of none is the printed text: half-rate everywhere.

name: The One-Man Band
historical_reference: Cymbals between the knees, harmonica in the rack — everything at once, none of it well
card_art: A figure buried in instruments, one drumstick raised hopefully
effect: Counts as THREE classes of your choice (chosen at acquisition) at one-third effect each.
pseudocode:
class SkillOneManBand extends CardModifierSkill:
  var tags : Array[StringName] = []  # picked at acquisition, saved on card
  func extra_class_tags() -> Array[StringName]: return tags
  func class_bonus_scale() -> float: return 0.334
why_fun: The affectionate anti-specialist — configure your own bad orchestra.
balance: Three thirds never equal one whole; he pads counts, not power.

name: The Comedic Drop
historical_reference: W.C. Fields — the fake fumble got bigger laughs than the trick
card_art: A juggler watching a dropped club bounce, grin spreading
effect: Cue: deliberately discard (drop) this card mid-show for a bonus LARGER than scoring it would have paid.
pseudocode:
class SkillComedicDrop extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    game.defer(func():
      game.add_line_score(row_of(data), 1.5 * base_points(data))
      game.discard_card(data)
      game.register_combo(combo_key()))
why_fun: Failure as a scored act — dropping the ball ON PURPOSE is the punchline.
balance: It pays once and leaves; the meld it abandoned scores without it.

name: Pierrot
historical_reference: The whitefaced moon-mad lover of French pantomime
card_art: A white figure gazing up at a moon that is a pale card
effect: Buffs at 'night': +mult on alternating shows (odd-numbered shows of the tour); pines uselessly otherwise.
why_fun: A rhythm the route planner can dance around — schedule your big towns by moonlight.
balance: Half your tour he is scenery.

name: Pagliacci
historical_reference: Vesti la giubba — the clown must perform while his heart breaks
card_art: A clown at a mirror, painted smile, real tears
effect: The show must go on: +mult while this card carries any debuff or negative status.
why_fun: Debuffs become fuel — the hazard town just handed you your best act.
balance: You must keep him hurt to keep him great; healing him nerfs him.

name: Fool's License
historical_reference: The court jester's license — the one voice permitted to mock the king
card_art: A jester bowing to a throne while pickpocketing the crown
effect: While spotlit: choose ONE rule card in the rules deck; its effect is ignored (the fool mocks the law).
why_fun: The cleanest possible use of the rule-deck architecture — one card that laughs at one law.
balance: Cover the fool and the law returns instantly, mid-act if it must.

name: The Vidushaka
historical_reference: Chakyar koothu — Kerala's temple jester, licensed to mock even kings mid-sermon
card_art: A jester whispering in the ear of a statue of the town's founder
effect: Once per tour: reduce the current show's declared goal (or Booking target) by 20% — the fool talks the crowd down.
pseudocode:
class SkillVidushaka extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if run_state().vidushaka_used: return
    run_state().vidushaka_used = true
    game.state.goal = game.state.goal.multiplied(0.8)  # BigNumber op
    game.register_combo(combo_key())
why_fun: An escape valve for the show you overbooked — mockery as mercy.
balance: One heckle per tour; save it wrong and the wall you hit is full height.

name: Whiteface & Auguste
historical_reference: Tom Belling tripped in the Berlin ring, 1869 — the crowd jeered 'August!' and a duo was born
card_art: An elegant whiteface pointing left while a rumpled Auguste falls right
effect: Pair card: cue the Whiteface to issue an order (choose an adjacent card's cue); the Auguste executes it WRONGLY — the effect fires on a different valid target, at 150%.
why_fun: Orders executed wrong for a BETTER result — comedy as a targeting system.
balance: You aim, the game misfires on purpose: 150% of the wrong thing is sometimes 0.

name: The Rodeo Clown
historical_reference: Rodeo bullfighters — comedy as armor, drawing the bull off fallen riders
card_art: A barrel-clad clown waving both arms at something enormous off-frame
effect: When a boss/town effect targets another of your cards, this card redirects the hit to itself (taunt, once per turn).
why_fun: A taunt verb wearing a red nose — your glass engine finally has a bodyguard.
balance: He eats every hit he steals; clowns are not durable.

name: Grock's Instrument
historical_reference: Grock — musical virtuosity forever defeated by furniture
card_art: A tiny violin on a huge piano lid, both slightly too far away
effect: This card refuses to score: each act it WOULD score, it instead hops one column away and banks the points. When finally cornered (no legal hop), it scores everything banked at once.
why_fun: A running gag with compound interest — you spend three turns herding the punchline.
balance: The bank pays only if you CATCH him; an open board is an endless chase.

name: Weary Willie
historical_reference: Emmett Kelly's Depression-era tramp — the clown who grew from hard times
card_art: A sad-faced tramp clown sweeping the spotlight into a dustpan
effect: Gains +1 rank permanently every time one of your cards is destroyed or a show is failed.
why_fun: Bad runs produce a souvenir — your losses literally accumulate into a performer.
balance: He grows from pain you did not want; a winning streak leaves him tiny.

name: Clown Car
historical_reference: Lou Jacobs' midget-car gag, Ringling, 1950s
card_art: A car the size of a shoe with fourteen elbows already out the window
effect: On Next: draw one extra card into this card's column, +1 more per Clown-class card scored last submit.
why_fun: An escalating tempo engine — the board literally overflows with clowns.
balance: Extra draws flood a board that Spotlight rules want SHALLOW; the car does not brake.

name: The Living Statue
historical_reference: Street living statues — paid by the hour for perfect stillness
card_art: A silver-painted figure on a crate, a coin frozen mid-toss before it
effect: Gains +points at every show end it was never moved this show; ANY grab resets the bonus to zero.
why_fun: Patience literally paid — the card you must consciously NOT touch.
balance: A card you cannot move is a slot you cannot fix.

name: The Charivari
historical_reference: The charivari — the clowns' opening tumbling rush that starts the show
card_art: A dozen tumblers pouring into the ring all at once, hats flying
effect: Turn-1 bonus: if you place 4+ cards into the Ring on the first turn of a show, +mult on the first act's payout.
why_fun: Rewards the opening FLOOD — turn one becomes a stampede by design.
balance: Dumping four cards fast means placing four cards badly.

name: Presentation
historical_reference: The user's Presentation card — ascending-order movement pays multiplier
card_art: A conveyor of cards each one rank higher, bowing in sequence
effect: While spotlit: each time you place a card whose rank is HIGHER than the last card you placed, +1 combo (U) for the act.
why_fun: Turns your HANDS into a combo meter — the order you play becomes choreography.
balance: Ascending order is a straitjacket when the board wants a king NOW.

name: Spiderweb
historical_reference: The user's Spiderweb card idea — diagonal cross reach
card_art: A dew-strung web spanning four diagonal cards from a center spider
effect: When scored: +1 point per card diagonal to this card (the X shape around it).
why_fun: The first card that makes DIAGONALS matter — a new axis appears in your head.
balance: Center placement is contested real estate; corners pay it almost nothing.

name: The Tango Pair
historical_reference: Tango — born dockside in Buenos Aires, perfected in the embrace
card_art: Two cards locked cheek to cheek mid-stride, one leg extended
effect: Pair: these two cards must always be adjacent (the free one slides to rejoin on Next); each turn they alternate who leads — only the leader's skill fires, at +50%.
why_fun: Two cards dancing across your board between turns — the embrace is a constraint you love.
balance: Two slots, one active skill at a time, and the follow drags cover with it.

name: Escaramuza
historical_reference: Escaramuza — Mexico's women's precision team riding, sidesaddle at full gallop
card_art: Eight riders crossing in perfect symmetry, dust in a mandala
effect: Symmetry bonus: when the board's column heights read the same left-to-right as right-to-left at scoring, +mult on the act.
why_fun: You start seeing the board as a mirror — a whole aesthetic becomes a payout.
balance: Symmetry fights greed: the best meld is usually asymmetric.

name: Twin Harmony
historical_reference: Violet and Daisy Hilton — conjoined-twin vaudeville stars, saxophone and jazz
card_art: Two performers sharing one silhouette, two saxophones raised
effect: Doubles any combo/multiplier effect on cards adjacent to it (auras, Spangles, chorus counts).
why_fun: A combo amplifier with an address — placement decides what gets doubled.
balance: Doubling nothing is nothing; she needs neighbors already worth doubling.

name: The Odd Couple
historical_reference: John Battersby (the Living Skeleton) married Hannah Perkins (the Fat Lady)
card_art: A wisp-thin card and an enormous card holding hands on a porch swing
effect: The LOWEST and HIGHEST ranked cards in this card's row buff each other (+points each) when the row scores.
why_fun: Rewards the row you were told not to build — the 2 and the King, together forever.
balance: A tight midrange row (the poker ideal) pays the couple least.

name: The Paired Giants
historical_reference: Anna Swan and Martin Bates — the giant couple, near fifteen feet together
card_art: Two towering cards ducking under the top of the frame, hands linked
effect: Pair: each gains +mult while BOTH are anywhere on the board.
why_fun: The simplest pair payoff in the game — draw them both and feel the room get taller.
balance: Half the pair is half a card; mulligans hate marriages.

name: Karagöz & Hacivat
historical_reference: The Turkish shadow-play duo — bickering across a lit screen for 500 years
card_art: Two shadow-puppets arguing from opposite sides of a glowing cloth
effect: Bound pair: each gets +mult while they are in OPPOSITE zones (one in the Entrance, one in the Ring) — they argue best apart.
why_fun: A pair you must keep APART — the anti-Tango, and the Entrance finally matters.
balance: Keeping one upstairs starves your Ring of a body every show.

name: The Kickline
historical_reference: The Moulin Rouge cancan — scandal as a chorus line
card_art: Five cards kicking in perfect unison, skirts of spades and hearts
effect: Full-row formation: when all five columns' TOP cards share a suit at scoring, the row above them scores again (the kickline encore).
why_fun: Five tops, one suit, whole line kicks — the board-wide costume check pays off.
balance: Suit-aligning five column TOPS fights every stacking rule in the book.

name: The Butoh Walker
historical_reference: Butoh — Japan's post-war dance of darkness, glacially slow, transformative
card_art: A white-painted figure mid-step, the floor behind it subtly changed
effect: Moves one slot (any direction) every OTHER turn automatically; every slot it leaves is permanently blessed: +points to the next card scored there.
why_fun: A slow tide that consecrates the board behind it — patience made visible.
balance: You do not steer it, and the blessing lands where IT walked, not where you play.

name: The Dragon Meld
historical_reference: Chinese dragon dance — a whole team animating one long body
card_art: A serpentine run of cards arched like a dragon between two poles
effect: When a run of 4+ scores while this card is in it, the run becomes a DRAGON: next turn it can be relocated as one body (moved anywhere as a unit, once).
why_fun: Your best run stops being furniture — the whole dragon walks to a better street.
balance: The dragon exists for one turn; hesitate and it is bricks again.

name: The Fadista
historical_reference: Fado and saudade — mourning what is gone as public art
card_art: A shawled singer, eyes closed, ghost-cards drifting past like memory
effect: Scores +N per card destroyed or lost this run — grief as accumulation.
why_fun: Every loss the run inflicted becomes her verse — the saddest scaling curve in the game.
balance: She starts worthless and grows only as your deck shrinks around her.

name: The Castell
historical_reference: Castells of Catalonia — human towers crowned by a child; força, equilibri, valor i seny
card_art: A trembling tower of cards with one tiny rank-1 card raising a hand at the top
effect: Tower formation: a stack of 6+ crowned by your LOWEST-ranked card pays a massive bonus at scoring; if the tower is broken by a hazard first, lose double the bonus.
why_fun: THE solitaire-stacking fantasy: build the tower, send the smallest card up, hold your breath.
balance: Six cards in one column crowned by your weakest — a hazard magnet with your name on it.

name: Straitjacket
historical_reference: Houdini's upside-down straitjacket escapes — performed free, in public, for crowds
card_art: A buckled canvas jacket straining, one sleeve already loose
effect: Starts each show unable to be moved; frees itself (and pays +points) the first time its column scores.
why_fun: A self-solving problem — the escape IS the payoff, every single show.
balance: Until it escapes it is a locked slot exactly where you placed it.

name: The Blockhead
historical_reference: Melvin Burkhart's human blockhead — comedy timing over gore
card_art: A grinning performer, hammer raised, nail glinting
effect: Absorbs one Knife prop per show: instead of being scored/hit, bank the knife as flat points.
why_fun: Standing in the knife lane on purpose — the flinch you do not make is the act.
balance: One nail per show; the second knife is just a knife.

name: The Target Girl
historical_reference: The impalement arts — the thrower gets billing, the nerve is hers
card_art: A poised assistant against a knife-ringed board, one blade still humming
effect: Placed in a Knife-prop row, converts every knife MISS into points (the miss bonus farms itself).
why_fun: Arguably the real star of the Knives archetype — she makes danger a salary.
balance: No knives, no paycheck: she is dead weight outside a Knife build.

name: Milk Can
historical_reference: Houdini's 'Failure Means a Drowning Death' milk-can escape
card_art: A riveted milk can, lid chained, one card-corner poking from the seam
effect: Cue: place one card from the board inside the can (out of play, safe from everything). Cue again any later turn to retrieve it to the Entrance.
why_fun: A pocket dimension for your combo piece — hold the ace through the storm.
balance: The held card does NOTHING while canned; tempo is the rent.

name: The Escape Map
historical_reference: WWII Bicycle decks hid escape maps for POWs — soak the card, peel it, assemble
card_art: A playing card peeling open to reveal an inked river and a border
effect: When discarded (soaked), reveals part of the world map's fog of war — sacrificial cartography.
why_fun: A card whose best play is its funeral — you spend a soldier to see the road.
balance: Discarding it is the effect; it never scores a point in its life.

name: The Mime
historical_reference: Marcel Marceau — who used mime to keep children silent while smuggling them across the Alps
card_art: A white-gloved figure, finger to lips, one hand shielding a small stack
effect: Silent escort: attaches to a stack; absorbs the first hostile event targeting any card in that stack (reverent flavor text mandatory).
why_fun: The guardian who says nothing — the history behind it earns the quiet.
balance: One interception, then he is spent and exposed himself.

name: The Invisible Wall
historical_reference: The mime's constructed nothing
card_art: A perfectly ordinary empty column that a strongman cannot enter
effect: Cue: block one EMPTY column with nothing — no card may enter or leave it for one turn (hazards included).
why_fun: Denying a column with mime hands — hazard routing made theatrical.
balance: It blocks YOUR plays there too, and only empty columns qualify.

name: The Double
historical_reference: Yakima Canutt — invented modern stunt craft so stars could seem immortal
card_art: Two identical cards mid-fall, one wearing a slightly better jacket
effect: Assign a 'star' card at show start: the first destruction or debuff aimed at the star hits this card instead.
why_fun: Your legendary gets a body man — the hit lands, the star walks.
balance: The double dies doing it, and he only guards ONE name on the call sheet.

name: Brodie's Leap
historical_reference: Steve Brodie claimed the Brooklyn Bridge jump, 1886 — famous anyway
card_art: A figure mid-leap over a painted bridge, the splash suspiciously dry
effect: Claims a feat: banks fame each show for a stunt that never happened. If ever inspected/exposed (Séance Buster, Prepared Town), lose DOUBLE everything banked.
why_fun: Free money on a lie that compounds — the exposure sword hangs heavier every show.
balance: The longer it works the more the debunk costs; Prepared Towns are a guillotine.

name: Edith's Bayonet
historical_reference: Edith Clifford swallowed a bayonet mounted on a rifle butt for Barnum & Bailey
card_art: A sword-swallower mid-swallow, rifle stock still visible
effect: Cue: swallow one Knives-suit card permanently (destroyed); its rank adds to hers FOREVER.
why_fun: Gluttony's dangerous cousin with a suit restriction and no take-backs.
balance: Every meal is a Knife card your knife build no longer owns.

name: The Fire Breather
historical_reference: Vaporized-fuel plumes — the least forgiving skill in the industry
card_art: A performer exhaling a rolling orange plume across five cards
effect: Cue: project Burning across this card's ENTIRE row (every card gains the status); this card gains Exhausted.
why_fun: A whole row set alight in one breath — the cascade build's ignition switch.
balance: He torches his own act to do it; Exhausted is not a costume.

name: The Regurgitator
historical_reference: Hadji Ali — swallowed water and kerosene, produced them separately, fire finale
card_art: A performer producing swallowed items in impossible order, audience recoiling
effect: Cue: swallow up to 3 board cards; produce them later to the Entrance in ANY order (a stack-order laundering machine).
why_fun: Order laundering — the sequencing sins of your board, absolved in one gulp.
balance: Three bodies off the board is three bodies not scoring.

name: The Hunger Artist
historical_reference: Giovanni Succi and the fasting fad; Kafka wrote the eulogy
card_art: A gaunt figure in a barred cage, tally marks scratched beside the door
effect: Gains +N for every consecutive show it stays in the deck UNPLAYED; when finally played and scored, pays the whole fast at once, then resets to zero.
why_fun: Patience literally scored — the longer you do not play your card, the louder its debut.
balance: A deck slot doing nothing for shows on end; drawn early, the fast breaks for pennies.

name: The Water Torture Cell
historical_reference: Houdini's upside-down, ankles-locked immersion escape
card_art: A card suspended head-down in a glass tank, three padlocks glowing
effect: Arrives locked under 3 conditions (covered / column unscored / not moved); one unlocks per turn in order; full escape pays a massive bonus.
why_fun: A three-turn countdown the whole board bends around — tension you can hear ticking.
balance: Three turns of vulnerability for one payout; a hazard mid-escape drowns the act.

name: Buried Alive
historical_reference: Fakir interment endurance; Houdini nearly died failing it in 1926
card_art: A coffin lid closing over a card, one corner of it still confident
effect: Cue: bury a card (removed from the game) for 2 shows; it returns UPGRADED one rarity (seeded chance it does not return at all).
why_fun: Send a card to the grave and bet on the resurrection — collection-level payoff, funeral-level stakes.
balance: Two shows without the card, and sometimes the coffin stays shut.

name: The Holdout
historical_reference: The Kepplinger sleeve machine — betrayed to the world by its inventor's friends
card_art: A mechanical brass arm easing a card out of a coat sleeve
effect: Store one card OUTSIDE the game (survives shows). Cue once per run: insert it into any stack at any moment. If a town searches you first, lose it.
why_fun: An ace up the sleeve — literally, mechanically, gloriously illegal.
balance: Search towns turn your insurance into a confession.

name: The Kneeling Devil
historical_reference: Diablos Danzantes de Yare — red devils who dance, then kneel at the church door
card_art: A masked red devil kneeling, mask half-lifted, dawn behind
effect: A negative card (debuffs its column each show) that, if kept in the deck for the FULL tour, converts at the final city into a Legendary blessing card.
why_fun: Carrying a curse the whole tour for one redemption — the longest bet on the map.
balance: A full tour of self-inflicted debuffs, and dropping it once voids everything.

name: Searing Knife
historical_reference: Adopted from the §14.5 summary list — column hazards enable town/boss content
card_art: A knife glowing forge-red buried point-down in a card column
effect: When scored: burns its own column — the column pays 3x this act but is LOCKED (no placement, no grabs) for the next 2 turns.
why_fun: Scorched-earth economics — triple now, cratered later, and you chose where.
balance: A locked column is a fifth of your board in ashes for two turns.

name: The Capoeirista
historical_reference: Capoeira — a martial art disguised as dance by enslaved Afro-Brazilians
card_art: A ginga stance flowing under a berimbau's curve, dust rising in the roda
effect: Registered as a Dancer; functions as an Escape Artist. In towns that ban combat/danger acts, performs anyway (bans read the registration).
why_fun: The crown jewel of the disguised-identity family — the ban is the buff.
balance: In friendly towns the disguise is just a smaller stat line.

name: The Wall of Death
historical_reference: Motordrome riders on vertical board walls
card_art: A motorcyclist horizontal on a wooden cylinder wall of card backs
effect: Aura: both adjacent columns score +mult, but every card in them takes 1 Exhausted debuff at show end (someone pays for the show).
why_fun: An aura with a bill attached — placement decides who pays it.
balance: The debuffs land on YOUR cards; the wall does not care who was load-bearing.

name: The Séance Buster
historical_reference: Late-life Houdini's crusade against fraudulent mediums
card_art: A flashlight beam catching cheesecloth ectoplasm mid-drop
effect: Cue: reveal ALL Gaff/Humbug/fake cards on the board (yours and the town's); +points per fake exposed.
pseudocode:
class SkillSeanceBuster extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var fakes : int = 0
    for c : CardData in game.state.board_cards():
      if is_gaff(c): reveal(c); fakes += 1
    game.add_line_score(row_of(data), BUST_PTS * fakes)
    game.register_combo(combo_key())
why_fun: Counterplay that makes fakes a real subgame — history's best rivalry, playable.
balance: It exposes YOUR Humbugs too; the flashlight does not check allegiance.

name: Bottom Deal
historical_reference: The card sharp's quiet arts — seconds and bottoms, never seen
card_art: A dealer's hand drawing smoothly from the deck's underside
effect: Cue: this turn, draws come from the BOTTOM of the deck instead of the top.
why_fun: One flick of the wrist and the Gold you sank comes up early — the deck has two doors now.
balance: One turn only, and the bottom is usually bottom for a reason.

name: X-Ray Eyes
historical_reference: Kuda Bux — the blindfolded fire-walker with 'X-ray eyes'
card_art: A blindfolded face turned upward, twin glows burning through the cloth
effect: While THIS card is covered (blinded), you can see all facedown and hidden cards on the board and the top 3 deck cards.
why_fun: A card that works best buried — burying becomes a play, not an accident.
balance: Uncover it and the sight goes dark; it never scores meaningfully itself.

name: The Tarot Reading
historical_reference: Carnival cartomancy — and a wink at Balatro's tarots
card_art: Five cards fanned face-down, one fingertip resting on the third
effect: Cue (once per show): look at the top 5 cards of the draw deck and reorder them freely.
pseudocode:
class SkillTarot extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.tarot_used: return
    game.state.tarot_used = true
    game.defer(func(): game.reorder_deck_top(cue_reorder_ui(5)))
why_fun: The scry every deck game needs — and here order IS position, so it is spatially loaded.
balance: Five cards of foresight, once; the sixth card is still fate.

name: The Second Sight
historical_reference: The Zancigs — 'Two Minds with but a Single Thought'
card_art: Two performers back to back, one blindfolded, both smiling
effect: Pair: while both cards are on the board, the top card of the draw deck is always revealed.
why_fun: Permanent deck-top vision changes every single Next decision — quiet and beloved.
balance: Two board slots for information that adds zero points directly.

name: Lucky Streak
historical_reference: The '+1 reroll per game; repeats will not repeat' note
card_art: A rabbit's foot on a chain, faintly glowing, slightly smug
effect: Grants +1 reroll per shop visit; and for the rest of any show after it activates, repeated actions never produce identical results (the RNG stream advances).
why_fun: Anti-frustration made diegetic — the game promises to never repeat the same cruelty twice.
balance: Luck-smoothing, not power: it improves your worst case only.

name: The Mulligan
historical_reference: The discards essay — 'this should probably be a joker actually'
card_art: A dealer's hand sweeping one card off the table, eyebrow raised
effect: Allows discarding 1 card from the Grand Entrance per turn (before it drops). Upgradable: further copies add +1 discard each.
why_fun: The gamble-for-a-better-hand loop the discards essay wanted — fun per click.
balance: Every mulligan feeds the discard pile the town may be reading.

name: Madame Zora's Eye
historical_reference: Boardwalk mitt camps and the Zoltar machine lineage
card_art: A neon fortune-teller booth, one glass eye glowing over the deck
effect: The next-drawn card is ALWAYS visible above the deck slot while this card is anywhere in play.
why_fun: Permanent information changes every plan — quiet, powerful, beloved.
balance: Information compounds only if you act on it; the eye never lifts a finger.

name: The Griot
historical_reference: West African griots — hereditary praise-singers, the living archive (absorbs the Storyteller card)
card_art: A robed singer with a kora, faint gold script streaming behind
effect: Preserves up to 20% of this show's unspent score into the next show's opening total, and re-triggers ONE effect of your choice from last show at the next show's start.
why_fun: Memory as an engine — last night's show opens tonight's.
balance: Carryover tempts you to sandbag a won show; the cap exists because of you.

name: Wax and Gold
historical_reference: Azmari sam-enna-warq — lyrics with a surface meaning and a hidden one, both true
card_art: A wax seal peeled back to reveal gold leaf lettering beneath
effect: Two rule texts: the visible 'wax' effect (+points when scored in a row meld) and a hidden 'gold' effect discovered only by fulfilling the wax one 5 times (then: +combo every meld).
why_fun: Double-meaning as design — the card literally rewards reading it twice.
balance: Five faithful scores before the real card shows up.

name: The Rider
historical_reference: Van Halen's brown M&Ms clause — the canary hidden in the contract
card_art: A contract page dense with clauses, one line faintly underlined
effect: Long rule text hiding ONE small instruction (e.g. 'never place me in column 3'). Obey it all show and this card scores DOUBLE; violate it and it scores zero, silently.
why_fun: An attention test disguised as flavor text — design's best inside joke, playable.
balance: Punishes skimming with silence; you find out at the payout.

name: The System
historical_reference: Derren Brown's guaranteed tipster — every branch filmed, only the winner shown
card_art: A wall of race slips, all losers hidden behind one glorious winner
effect: After each act scores, this card 'predicted' it: +points scaled to the act's combo (retroactive prophecy). Any inspection effect reveals the trick and halves it permanently.
why_fun: It always knew — free money that dares anyone to look behind the curtain.
balance: Knowledge costs: one Learned Pig, yours or the town's, halves it forever.

name: The Spirit Cabinet
historical_reference: The Davenport brothers — tied inside, instruments played 'by spirits'
card_art: A wooden cabinet with instruments floating out of its seams
effect: While this card is FULLY covered, its whole column's card effects trigger as if spotlit ('the spirits' do it).
why_fun: An entire buried column playing itself — the séance is a wiring diagram.
balance: Uncover the cabinet and every 'spirit' in the column goes silent at once.

name: The Cliffhanger
historical_reference: P.C. Sorcar's 1956 BBC sawing ran past the cutoff — the nation believed her dead overnight
card_art: A sawing act frozen at the worst possible moment, broadcast static creeping in
effect: Cue at show end: end the show mid-trick — the next town's goal drops 30% (everyone is talking), but you MUST 'resolve the trick' there: this card must score in that show or lose double the discount as fame.
why_fun: Manufactured suspense with a due date — the tour itself becomes serialized drama.
balance: The resolution is mandatory; failing the payoff show costs more than the discount gave.

name: Drumroll
historical_reference: Every circus trick ever — the snare before the catch
card_art: Two drumsticks blurred above a snare, spotlight tightening
effect: The LAST meld scored each act gets +mult (the finish lands harder).
why_fun: Trigger order matters again — you save the best for last on purpose.
balance: Only the final meld; a scattered act wastes the snare.

name: The Sparkler
historical_reference: Handheld fireworks — the pocket-sized spectacle
card_art: A single sparkler fizzing gold over one card corner
effect: When this card scores, bank +10 flat points to its COLUMN score (a small firework).
why_fun: The first taste of the col-points economy — small, bright, reliable.
balance: Ten points is ten points; it never scales.

name: Sliced Bread
historical_reference: The user's Sliced Bread card — the best thing since
card_art: A loaf of cards mid-slice, one same-suit slice glowing deeper in
effect: On submission: if a same-suit card sits lower in this card's stack, gain a point per card between them (look-ahead scan).
why_fun: Pays you for the sandwich you built — stack archaeology as scoring.
balance: Wants same-suit spacing that stacking rules actively forbid.

name: Poker Crown
historical_reference: The user's card — 'this card and the 4 above it score as a poker hand'
card_art: A five-card crown assembling above one proud card
effect: When scored: this card and the 4 cards directly above it in its column are ALSO evaluated as a poker hand (a vertical hand, once).
why_fun: A private Symmetrical Show — one column learns to play poker.
balance: Needs 4 real cards stacked above it: coverage IS the cost.

name: Column Poker
historical_reference: The user's card — every 5 cards per column scores as a poker hand
card_art: A column ruled into five-card bands, each band a fanned hand
effect: While spotlit: every full 5-card band of this card's column is evaluated as a poker hand at scoring.
why_fun: Tall columns become poker ladders — depth finally speaks the row's language.
balance: Ten cards deep to double-dip; the spotlight economy pays for every floor.

name: The Calliope
historical_reference: The steam calliope — heard miles before the circus arrived
card_art: Brass pipes venting steam, each whistle a different suit color
effect: +1 combo (U) for every DIFFERENT class that scored this act.
why_fun: Directly pays the rainbow board — the anti-hyperfocus card the optimization essay asked for.
balance: A tight one-archetype deck hears one whistle.

name: The Kite
historical_reference: Wau bulan — Malaysia's singing moon-kites
card_art: A crescent kite trailing a humming string up out of the board frame
effect: Cue: launch a card above the board (out of play, singing). Choose the turn it returns: it lands with +rank equal to turns aloft (max 3).
why_fun: A card that literally gains value hanging in the sky — patience with a string on it.
balance: Aloft is absent: the wind holds your card while the show needs it.

name: The Steelpan
historical_reference: Trinidad — drums banned, so oil barrels became a symphony
card_art: A shining oil-drum pan hammered full of card pips
effect: Cue (once per show): permanently convert one prop (skill-less) card into an instrument card: it gains this skill — +points when its row melds.
why_fun: The most triumphant member of the ban-workaround family — trash becomes orchestra, permanently.
balance: One conversion per show, and the note it grants is a small one.

name: The Boot Code
historical_reference: Gumboot dance — miners forbidden to speak invented a slap-and-stamp language
card_art: A line of gumboots mid-stamp, rhythm lines passing boot to boot
effect: Passes a buff down its row card-to-card by rhythm WITHOUT triggering (invisible to effects that count or punish triggers, e.g. Patience, trigger-tax bosses).
why_fun: Untraceable value under trigger-hostile rules — forbidden language as tech.
balance: Silent buffs also skip your own trigger-payoff engines (Brass Check hears nothing).

name: The Fanchant
historical_reference: K-pop fanchants — the crowd answering the stage in synchronized script
card_art: An audience of card backs shouting a glowing call-and-response bar
effect: After all scoring resolves, each meld that raised U this act ECHOES: pays again at 25% (the crowd answers).
why_fun: Everything good you did, the crowd does back — variety pays twice.
balance: Echoes only U-raising melds: dumping duplicates earns silence.

name: Avalanche
historical_reference: The user's Avalanche card — the board attacks the scoreboard
card_art: A wall of cards crashing down onto a giant painted score counter
effect: After scoring, all cards on the board 'attack the scoreboard': reduce the show's GOAL by the number of cards on board.
why_fun: Attacking the requirement instead of the score — the goalpost finally bleeds.
balance: Scales with board SIZE, so it fights every clear-the-board scoring instinct.

name: The Ninot
historical_reference: Las Fallas — a year building the sculpture, one night burning it; one figure pardoned
card_art: A magnificent papier-mâché figure of a card, torch approaching
effect: Event-granted: invest points into this card each show (cue: bank any amount of the act's score). At the final city, burn it: pays triple everything invested. Public vote (seeded) may pardon it — it survives to next run.
why_fun: Build-to-burn across a whole tour — the finale is a bonfire you fed all season.
balance: Every point banked is a point not scoring shows NOW.

name: The Drone Swarm
historical_reference: Drone light shows — thousands of drones drawing on the night sky
card_art: A constellation of pinpoint lights sketching a card formation in darkness
effect: Cue (once per show): re-project any formation bonus you have ALREADY scored this run (Pyramid, Castell, Kickline...) — it pays again now, without the cards.
pseudocode:
class SkillDroneSwarm extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.swarm_used: return
    game.state.swarm_used = true
    var f : StringName = pick_from(run_state().formations_scored)
    game.add_line_score(0, formation_payout(f))
    game.register_combo(combo_key())
why_fun: The Firework suit's modern evolution — your greatest hits, replayed in lights.
balance: It can only replay what you already earned; an empty highlight reel projects nothing.

name: Grand Spec
historical_reference: The 'spec' — the whole company's opening spectacle parade
card_art: Every performer of the show crossing the ring at once, banners high
effect: If ALL columns scored a meld this act, double the act's combo multiplier.
why_fun: The perfect-show fantasy button — five columns, one parade, double everything.
balance: One silent column and the parade never happens.

name: Duende
historical_reference: Lorca's duende — when performance stops being technique and becomes possession
card_art: A dancer mid-move, the whole frame dark except one impossible spotlight
effect: Emergent state, never directly cued: when an act scores 5+ distinct combo classes AND every meld this act raised U, this card transcends — the act's payout triples, the screen dims to one spotlight.
why_fun: Never purchasable, only summoned — the run where it fires becomes the run you talk about.
balance: Perfect variety under pressure; chasing duende deliberately is how you miss it.

name: Peanut Pitch
historical_reference: Peanut butchers working the grandstand aisles
card_art: A paper sack of peanuts arcing over three rows of card heads
effect: Cue (3 charges per show): +points to any ONE card, thrown from anywhere on the board.
pseudocode:
class SkillPeanutPitch extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_game_start() -> void: game.state.charges[data] = 3
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.charges[data] <= 0: return
    game.state.charges[data] -= 1
    buff_card(cue_target(), PEANUT_PTS)
    game.register_combo(combo_key())
why_fun: The charge system's tutorial card — three throws, anywhere, always useful.
balance: Small numbers thrice; it fixes nothing structural.

name: Fairy Floss
historical_reference: Cotton candy — co-invented by a dentist, William Morrison, 1897
card_art: A pink cloud of floss half-wrapping the neighboring cards
effect: When scored: sweeten all adjacent cards — +1 rank for the rest of the show (the dentist thanks you).
why_fun: Adjacency buffs turn placement into frosting — and the dentist fact belongs in the flavor text.
balance: Rank +1 can BREAK a planned pair or run as easily as it helps.

name: Pink Lemonade
historical_reference: Circus legend: red tights washed in the lemonade barrel — and it sold better
card_art: A suspiciously pink glass raised in a toast beside a flame
effect: When a Flames card and a Concessions card score in the same row: +bonus points and draw 1 card.
why_fun: Teaches cross-class rows early — the 'aha' card of the first hour.
balance: Needs two specific ingredients in one row; no accident pays it.

name: Popcorn Cascade
historical_reference: Concession-stand physics — one kernel is never one kernel
card_art: A popcorn box erupting in slow motion over a scoring column
effect: Whenever a column of 4+ cards scores, draw 1 card (the pop feeds the crowd feeds the pop).
why_fun: Card advantage bolted to tall play — the draw engine that wants towers.
balance: Tall columns bury spotlights; the popcorn does not care.

name: The Grease Joint
historical_reference: Circus cookhouse slang — where the lot actually eats
card_art: A sizzling flat-top grill, one card-shaped burger flipping
effect: At show start: feed the BOTTOM card of the draw deck to the discard, then draw 1 (deck-bottom filtering no other slot touches).
why_fun: Quietly eats the card you were never going to reach anyway.
balance: Blind filtering: someday the bottom card was your Final Act closer.

name: The Candy Butcher
historical_reference: Aisle vendors — 'candy butchers' worked the stands mid-show
card_art: A tray-strapped vendor flipping a Ticket Stub like a coin
effect: Whenever a full 5-card row meld scores, mint one Ticket Stub to the deck (sales spike at the big moments).
why_fun: The economy that pays YOU for playing well — commerce rides the applause.
balance: Stubs are shop-only value and deck dilution until spent.

name: Cheat Day
historical_reference: The user's Cheat Day card — win-fast rewards as card effects
card_art: A gilded calendar page marked with a fork and knife
effect: If the show is won with cards still in the draw deck: every card remaining in the deck gains +1 rank permanently.
why_fun: Winning FAST finally pays — the leftover deck comes back stronger next town.
balance: Rewards ending early, which fights every squeeze-the-board instinct you have.

name: Meal Call
historical_reference: The cookhouse flag — when it flew, the lot ate
card_art: A flag rising over a steaming mess tent, cards queuing with trays
effect: Once per show (cue): every card on the board heals one debuff/status (the day's heartbeat).
why_fun: A whole-board deep breath — hazard towns lose their sting for one flag.
balance: One meal per show, and Pagliacci decks resent the kindness.

name: The Incubator
historical_reference: Dr. Couney's Coney Island incubator sideshow funded care hospitals refused — thousands of children saved
card_art: A gentle glass cabinet glowing warm, tiny cards sleeping inside
effect: Exhibited healing: cards COVERED by this card recover — each turn covered, remove one debuff and restore 1 lost rank ('on display' is the cure).
why_fun: The best true story in the whole compendium — a sideshow that heals, and covering becomes kindness.
balance: It must sit ON the patients: the healer blocks their spotlight while they mend.

name: The Tailor's Dummy
historical_reference: Wardrobe department stock — the dress form that wears everything
card_art: A headless dress form wearing two magnificent stamped sashes
effect: Prop card (no skill possible): can hold TWO stamps.
why_fun: A cheap body for your best two stamps — the wardrobe build's load-bearing mannequin.
balance: Two stamps on a card that can never act; support squared, output zero.

name: The Costume Fitting
historical_reference: Wardrobe mistresses of the great shows — everyone fitted before curtain
card_art: A tape measure curling around a card mid-fitting, pins hovering
effect: On show start: this card copies the stamp of one adjacent card for the duration of the show.
why_fun: Borrowed finery — one good stamp quietly becomes two, every show.
balance: Copies whatever is adjacent at curtain, not what you WISH was adjacent.

name: Hikinuki
historical_reference: Kabuki hikinuki — costumes ripped away in one motion mid-dance
card_art: A costume tearing away mid-spin to reveal a different color beneath
effect: On scoring: swap this card's stamp with any stamp held in reserve (the quick-change happens in the applause).
why_fun: Mid-show stamp rotation — one card wears the whole wardrobe across three acts.
balance: Needs a stamp reserve stocked in advance; a bare closet quick-changes into nothing.

name: The Wardrobe Trunk
historical_reference: The great touring trunks — a season's costumes in one box
card_art: A steamer trunk exploding with sashes, seals, and one escaping hat
effect: Holds one stamp in reserve. Cue: apply the reserved stamp to any spotlit card (permanent); the trunk then refills at the next shop.
why_fun: Stamps become a deployable resource — the trunk decides who dresses up tonight.
balance: One stamp in the pipe, refill gated behind shops.

name: Hanafuda Reskin
historical_reference: Hanafuda — every gambling ban spawned a new disguised deck; Nintendo was founded to print them
card_art: A card mid-transformation from pips to painted flowers
effect: Ban evasion: when a town outlaws a suit, all your cards of that suit RESKIN (count as the suit of the adjacent card) for that show.
why_fun: The ban-evasion family's founding legend — the law changes, the deck redraws itself.
balance: Only suit bans, only that show; and reskinned suits fire no props.

name: The Mask Maker
historical_reference: Venetian mascherari — the guild that made anonymity wearable
card_art: A workbench of half-finished masks, one turned toward the viewer
effect: Cue (once per show): apply a Masked status to any card — its identity/rarity is hidden from inspections and towns until it scores twice.
pseudocode:
class SkillMaskMaker extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.mask_used: return
    game.state.mask_used = true
    var st : StatusMasked = StatusMasked.new()  # duplicate per application
    game.defer(func(): cue_target().add_status(st))
why_fun: On-demand anonymity — the Bauta stamp as a service, aimed where the inspector looks.
balance: Two scores and the mask slips; timing the reveal is on you.

name: Gold Card
historical_reference: The user's gold cards — heavy tokens, cards as currency
card_art: A thick gold-leafed card, edges worn from changing hands
effect: Money token (Producer-minted): full trade value at shops; TypeHeavy — always sinks to the deck bottom; scores 0 in melds.
pseudocode:
# token card: TypeHeavy + no skill; minted by effects, spent at shops
# construction site (any minting effect):
var gold : CardData = CardData.new()
gold.type = TypeHeavy.new()
gold.rank = RULES_GOLD  # trade value read by shop layer
GameData.relink_card_backrefs(gold)
why_fun: Money that takes up physical deck space — wealth literally weighs your draws down.
balance: Every coin is a dead draw; hoarders drown in their own treasury.

name: The Cheap Pop
historical_reference: 'It's great to be here in [YOUR TOWN]!' — instant applause, every night
card_art: A performer arms wide before a banner reading the current town's name
effect: When scored: +small bonus fame in EVERY town (it names the town; the crowd always cheers). Universally decent, never great — the design lesson in card form.
why_fun: It always works, everywhere, forever — the comfort-food card.
balance: The ceiling IS the floor; no town ever pays it extra.

name: Common
historical_reference: The user's rarity-chain — Common doubles Rare chances
card_art: A plain grey card holding open a velvet door slightly too grand for it
effect: While in your deck: chances of Rare cards appearing in packs/shops are doubled.
why_fun: The ladder's first rung — a common whose whole job is summoning better company.
balance: A dead card in every show; it only ever works between them.

name: The Hat Line
historical_reference: 'If you liked the show...' — the busker's ask is a craft of its own
card_art: An upturned hat mid-toss catching a glittering arc of coins
effect: At show end (cue): convert the act's leftover combo (unused U above the payout) into Gold tokens instead of nothing.
why_fun: Nothing goes to waste — showmanship that overflows becomes rent money.
balance: Minted gold is deck weight; the hat feeds the same greed it monetizes.

name: Exchange Voucher
historical_reference: The user's Exchange Voucher — origin of 'all buttons should be cards'
card_art: An official-looking voucher with a dotted line and one waiting scissors
effect: Usable from the DECK (global): cue to swap this card with any card on screen — positions exchanged exactly.
pseudocode:
class SkillVoucher extends CardModifierSkill:
  # StampGlobal-style activation: cueable from inside the deck
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var t : CardData = cue_target()
    game.defer(func():
      game.swap_positions_any_stage(data, t)
      game.register_combo(combo_key())
why_fun: A teleporter disguised as paperwork — and the ancestor of every button-as-card idea.
balance: The voucher takes the swapped card's place: your problem changes address, not size.

name: The Egress
historical_reference: Barnum's 'This Way to the Egress' — the exit sold as an exhibit
card_art: An ornate sign pointing grandly at a plain door
effect: Cue: send any card on the board straight to the discard; gain 1 Ticket Stub (the crowd follows the sign).
pseudocode:
class SkillEgress extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    game.defer(func():
      game.discard_card(cue_target())
      game.mint_card(TypeTicketStub))
    game.register_combo(combo_key())
why_fun: Targeted removal plus a punchline that lands every single time it is used.
balance: Discarding your own performer for pocket change is usually exactly that.

name: First of May
historical_reference: 'First of May' — circus slang for a first-season rookie
card_art: A bright-eyed young card with a bindle, name written in wet ink
effect: Event-granted rookie: a random Common with a hidden growth condition — if it scores 10 times across the run, it upgrades permanently to its Rare version.
why_fun: Watching the rookie earn the upgrade is a whole season of sports movie in one card.
balance: Ten scores is a real investment in a card that starts Common.

name: Humbug
historical_reference: 'The Prince of Humbugs' — Barnum's own proud title; the Fiji Mermaid
card_art: A magnificent silhouette on a poster; below it, a small dried fish in a case
effect: While COVERED: counts as a copy of the most valuable card in its row. When spotlit (uncovered): revealed as junk — rank 2, scores base only.
why_fun: A card you WANT buried — inverts spotlight and gets a laugh at every reveal.
balance: One careless uncovering converts your star into a dried fish.

name: White Elephant
historical_reference: The Barnum–Forepaugh White Elephant War of 1884 — Forepaugh's was painted
card_art: A pale elephant wearing a price tag, both magnificent and ruinous
effect: Huge points when scored; but each show it stays in your deck, it eats 1 Gold token (or 1 rank of your richest card if you have none).
why_fun: 'When do I finally dump it' is a real decision every single town.
balance: A cost-over-time bomb: hesitation compounds, and selling it admits defeat.

name: The Advance Campaign
historical_reference: Barnum pre-sold Jenny Lind to a nation that had never heard her sing
card_art: A wall of posters for a show that has not arrived, crowd already queuing
effect: Cue before a show (map layer): spend N Gold tokens; that show's fame payout is multiplied by 1 + N/10 (hype as investment).
why_fun: Buying your own hype and then having to live up to it — the Barnum loop, playable.
balance: Spent gold is gone whether or not the show delivers.

name: The Sponsorship
historical_reference: Red Bull Stratos — the patron's logo on every frame of the miracle
card_art: A magnificent leap framed entirely inside a corporate logo border
effect: A patron funds the tour: gain 2 Gold tokens every show start — but ad stamps occupy one stamp slot on your two highest cards (fame tax: -10% payouts).
why_fun: Guaranteed income with a visible cost stitched onto your best performers.
balance: The logo eats real stamp slots and skims every payout; leaving the deal mid-tour is not offered.

name: The Dubplate
historical_reference: Jamaican soundclash — exclusive dubplates no rival sound system can own
card_art: An acetate disc cut with YOUR name, still warm from the lathe
effect: Event-forged exclusive: a custom card created for THIS save file only (you name it; its skill is assembled from two effects you scored most this run).
why_fun: A card that exists nowhere else on Earth — your run history pressed into acetate.
balance: It is forged from what you ALREADY do most: power, yes; surprise, never.

name: Rare
historical_reference: The user's rarity-chain — Rare doubles Epic chances
card_art: A silvered card holding open a gilt door, posture improving
effect: While in your deck: chances of Epic cards appearing in packs/shops are doubled.
why_fun: Rung two of the ladder — the collection habit, compounding.
balance: Another show-dead card; the chain is a tax the meta layer collects.

name: Press Bribery
historical_reference: The user's Press Bribery / Memory Wipe card
card_art: A newspaper editor pocketing an envelope, tomorrow's headline already kind
effect: Extra life: on failing a city, keep touring as if you won (once). If still unspent at the final city, it converts to an automatic win there instead.
why_fun: An extra life you might deliberately never use — the save-it-for-the-end tension is exquisite.
balance: Held too long it insures shows you would have won anyway; spent early it is gone at the wall.

name: The Red Wagon
historical_reference: The red wagon — the circus office and ticket wagon: where the money lives
card_art: A scarlet wagon with a barred window, gold light leaking through the slats
effect: While spotlit: all Gold token cards in your DECK count as wildcards toward every column run (the treasury performs).
why_fun: THE pivot card — a money-hoarding build wakes up one morning as a scoring build.
balance: Everything hinges on one spotlit card; cover the wagon and your wealth is just weight again.

name: Epic
historical_reference: The user's rarity-chain — Epic doubles Legendary chances
card_art: A gilt card bowing before a door made entirely of light
effect: While in your deck: chances of Legendary cards appearing in packs/shops are doubled.
why_fun: The last rung before the top of the ladder — anticipation as a card.
balance: Three deck slots (the full chain) buy odds, never certainty.

name: Eye of God
historical_reference: The user's god-cycle — true vision
card_art: An immense calm eye opening above the board, decks rendered as glass
effect: See EVERYTHING: all decks (draw order included), the hidden rules deck, and the true descriptions of every card (Gaffs, Humbugs, Riders exposed).
why_fun: Total information — the game with the curtain torn away is a different, colder, wonderful game.
balance: It changes no card and adds no point; omniscience still has to play the board.

name: Hand of God
historical_reference: The user's god-cycle — the moving hand
card_art: A colossal gentle hand descending, one fingertip on a single card
effect: Cue: move any card to ANY location on the board (legality ignored), or reorder the cards inside any deck.
why_fun: Absolute spatial authority — every locked board opens to the Hand.
balance: One cue per activation and it fixes placement, not points.

name: Foot of God
historical_reference: The user's god-cycle — the switching foot
card_art: A vast bare foot poised above the board, one card already trading places with it
effect: Cue: this card switches locations with ANY card on screen — board, Entrance, decks, anywhere.
pseudocode:
class SkillFootOfGod extends CardModifierSkill:
  # StampGlobal-style activation: switches even from inside a deck
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    game.defer(func():
      game.swap_positions_any_stage(data, cue_target())
      game.register_combo(combo_key())
why_fun: The teleporting deity — position stops being fate anywhere on screen.
balance: It moves ITSELF, not others: divine mobility, mortal statline.

name: The Pawn
historical_reference: The user's chess cards — the pawn's march
card_art: A carved pawn edging up a column of cards, shadow longer than it is
effect: Freakshow (chess): if stacked under a card, swaps upward with it on Next (2 slots if bottommost). If placed diagonally-adjacent upward onto a card, CAPTURES: replaces it (the card is discarded).
why_fun: A card with a gait — it walks your column while you play the rest of the board.
balance: Slow, single-minded, and its capture discards YOUR card too if you misread the diagonal.

name: The Knight
historical_reference: The user's chess cards — pieces stack by chess law
card_art: A knight piece mid-leap over two stacked cards, mane of card slivers
effect: Freakshow (chess): may be placed onto any card an L-shape away (2+1), discarding and replacing it (capture). Ignores stacking legality.
why_fun: The only piece that jumps — covered cards stop being safe from you.
balance: Every landing kills the card you land on; the L rarely points where you wish.

name: The Bishop
historical_reference: The user's chess cards
card_art: A mitred piece sliding along a glowing diagonal of card corners
effect: Freakshow (chess): may be placed onto any card along an open diagonal, capturing it. Blocked by occupied slots between.
why_fun: Long-range diagonal threat turns the whole board into sightlines.
balance: Diagonals clog fast on a five-wide board; a full board blinds it.

name: The Rook
historical_reference: The user's chess cards
card_art: A tower piece grinding straight down a column, dust of pips rising
effect: Freakshow (chess): may be placed onto any card along its open row or column, capturing it.
why_fun: Straight-line dominance — the file you leave open is the file it owns.
balance: Every capture is also a discard from your own board economy.

name: The Queen
historical_reference: The user's chess cards
card_art: A crowned piece radiating open lines in eight directions
effect: Freakshow (chess): may be placed onto any card along any open line or diagonal, capturing it.
why_fun: The board's apex predator — nowhere uncovered is out of reach.
balance: She eats your OWN cards to move; an all-powerful piece with a body count you pay for.

name: The King
historical_reference: The user's chess cards — the swap-only King, ton of points if captured
card_art: A king piece shuffling one square sideways, crown slightly too heavy
effect: Freakshow (chess): moves like a king but only SWAPS (never captures); auto-steps out of 'check' (any chess card's capture line) whenever you act. If a chess card ever captures him: the captor gains a TON of points and +1 rank (hippo-style cap on captures).
why_fun: A live chess puzzle inside solitaire — hunting your own King with your own pieces is the alt-win-con toy the notes wanted.
balance: He dodges every time you act; the regicide payout demands a real two-piece mate.

name: The Prompter
historical_reference: The prompter's box — the whisperer sunk into the stage floor, feeding lines
card_art: A head poking from a floor hatch, script open, lips mid-whisper
effect: While spotlit: choose one card anywhere in this card's column — that card's modifiers are ACTIVE regardless of covering (its lines are fed from the box).
pseudocode:
# SEAM: activation is a per-card question (is_active) — this makes it a GRAPH.
class SkillPrompter extends CardModifierSkill:
  var client : CardData  # chosen on placement
  func spotlight_grants() -> Array[CardData]:
    return [client] if col_of(client) == col_of(data) else []
  # CardModifier.is_active must consult every active card's spotlight_grants —
  # activation becomes a query over OTHER modifiers, not a local property
why_fun: Activation stops being a property and becomes a relationship — the first card whose text is about another card's text.
balance: The client is only lit while the Prompter is; one cover breaks two cards.

name: The Metronome
historical_reference: Maelzel's metronome — the tempo machine whose maker also toured the Turk
card_art: A brass metronome ticking over the board, every halo pulsing in time
effect: All charge-based effects on your cards get +1 maximum charge (the shared charge system, tuned faster).
pseudocode:
# SEAM: charges are ONE shared system on GameData (the notes demanded this) —
# so one card can retune every consumer of it at once.
class SkillMetronome extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_active() -> void: game.state.charge_cap_bonus = 1
  func on_deactive() -> void: game.state.charge_cap_bonus = 0
  # every charge grantor reads: base_charges(card) + state.charge_cap_bonus
why_fun: One tick of the metronome and every Peanut Pitch, Tap Clown, and Tumbler in the deck plays a longer bar.
balance: Worthless in a deck with no charge cards — it modifies a system, not a board.

name: The Cue Card
historical_reference: Stagehands' cue cards — the act does what the card says, when the card says
card_art: A stagehand holding up an enormous card that just says NOW
effect: Cue: manually fire one adjacent card's on_trigger hook, exactly as if the game had triggered it (attribution, combo credit and stamps included).
why_fun: Any card with a trigger becomes a button — the dispatch system itself is now a player verb.
balance: It spends your cue to fire someone else's act; a deck of passives has no buttons to press.

name: Extra Point
historical_reference: The original deck5 tester card — the first skill Solatro ever ran
card_art: A single glowing +10 chalked on a slate beside the ring
effect: Gain extra points on score (currently +10 total).
why_fun: The baseline every other effect is measured against — the catalog's control group.
balance: Ten flat points never scale; it exists to be outgrown.

name: Echoing Trigger
historical_reference: The canyon echo — every showman's second house
card_art: A shout leaving a mouth twice, the second copy slightly translucent
effect: ALL triggers on the board repeat once (once per card per scoring pass) — a global echo on the event bus.
why_fun: Every effect in your deck fires twice — the whole engine, doubled by one card.
balance: It echoes hazard triggers and dud triggers with the same enthusiasm.

name: The Dragoman
historical_reference: The touring company's dragoman — the interpreter every foreign engagement hired
card_art: An interpreter standing between two cards, translating gestures mid-air
effect: Choose a card: its on_score effects ALSO fire when it is discarded (its act translated into a language the discard pile understands).
why_fun: Hook remapping as a player verb — the event bus grows a translation layer.
balance: A translated effect fires with a synthetic context; some scores read as gibberish in exile.

name: The Contagion
historical_reference: The wave — one pair of hands, then the whole grandstand
card_art: A ripple of color passing seat to seat through a crowd of card backs
effect: Each Next, every status on this card's neighbors copies one card further outward (statuses spread by adjacency, merge-by-class capping the counts).
why_fun: Your Burning cascade stops needing fire cards — the board becomes its own weather system.
balance: It spreads Exhausted and Muddy exactly as happily as it spreads your buffs.

name: The Alchemist
historical_reference: Medicine-show transmutation — lead into gold, nightly, allegedly
card_art: A robed vendor pouring one glowing status vial into a differently glowing one
effect: Cue: transmute ALL statuses of one class on a target card into another class, count preserved (Burning into Juggling, Exhausted into Sweetened...).
why_fun: Status classes become a currency with an exchange window — debuff decks learn alchemy.
balance: Transmutation is symmetric: fumble the direction and you just gilded a curse.

name: The Net
historical_reference: The safety net — the invention that turned death acts into repeatable art
card_art: A taut rope net catching a cannonball prop mid-arc, re-tensioning to throw
effect: Once per act: catch a ballistic prop (Ball/Fire) mid-flight and re-aim it at any card in the same column (the mancala targets were a suggestion).
why_fun: Prop trajectories stop being physics and start being negotiable — the catch IS the act.
balance: One catch per act, same column only; the net does not reach across the ring.

name: The Prop Master
historical_reference: The theatre property master — nothing flies that the prop table did not issue
card_art: A backstage table of hoops, knives, balls and flames, all labeled, one extra of each
effect: Every PropSpawner your suits produce gets +1 remaining (one extra prop per volley, every volley).
why_fun: Every suit in the deck quietly fires heavier — a whole archetype tuned by one hire.
balance: Plus-one props also means plus-one chaos in every act you did not plan.

name: The Exchange Rate
historical_reference: World's-fair money changers — every currency, a spread on each
card_art: A teller's window where combo sparks trade against stacks of flat points
effect: At act end (cue): convert unused combo — each U above 3 — into flat points at a fixed rate, or pay points to pre-register +1 U for the NEXT act.
why_fun: The multiplier becomes a market — sandbagging and banking U is a whole new axis of play.
balance: Every conversion is a spread paid; the teller always wins a little.

name: The Stage Manager
historical_reference: The SM's prompt book — the show runs in the order the book says, not the script
card_art: A clipboard opened over the rules deck, tabs being reordered mid-performance
effect: Cue (once per show): reorder the rules deck — changing which rule answers FIRST for grabs, placement, and scoring disputes.
pseudocode:
# SEAM: first-implementer-wins precedence 'depends on board order' (§8 sharp
# edge) — this card makes that landmine a deliberate player control.
class SkillStageManager extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    if game.state.sm_used: return
    game.state.sm_used = true
    game.reorder_rules_deck(cue_order_ui())  # Game deck fn; bump revision
    # implementer cache keys on [state id, revision] — reorder invalidates it
why_fun: Dispatch precedence — the deepest invisible system in the engine — handed to the player as a clipboard.
balance: Reordering laws you do not fully understand is exactly as dangerous as it sounds.

name: The Critic
historical_reference: One review from Shaw could close a show overnight
card_art: A monocled figure in the front row, pen scratching, acts wilting in its shadow
effect: After each act: the effect that triggered MOST is silenced for the next act (overexposed); any effect that triggered exactly once scores a bonus now (fresh, the critic approves).
why_fun: The engine deck's natural predator and the variety deck's best friend — a review, every act.
balance: Your best engine gets benched at its peak, by your own hire.

name: The Conductor
historical_reference: Merle Evans — Ringling's bandmaster for fifty years; the band followed HIS eyes
card_art: A bandmaster's baton frozen at the top of the beat, every card watching it
effect: All your on_next effects are HELD each turn and fire together at Submit instead, in an order you choose (the band comes in on the downbeat).
why_fun: Retiming an entire hook class turns tempo itself into a build — draw engines learn syncopation.
balance: A held on_next is a draw you did NOT get this turn; the downbeat had better be worth it.

name: The Rigger
historical_reference: Circus riggers — the unseen crew who decide what flies and what falls
card_art: Two harnesses crossed backstage, each act now wearing the other's ropes
effect: Cue: swap the WIRING of two cards — each card's skill now answers the other card's events (A's skill fires on B's scores, B's on A's).
pseudocode:
# SEAM: a modifier's data backref IS its event identity — cross-wiring means
# two proxies whose forwarded calls carry the other card's context.
class SkillRigger extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var a : CardData = cue_target_a(); var b : CardData = cue_target_b()
    a.skill = ProxyRemap.crosswire(a.skill, b)  # decorator system (Dragoman)
    b.skill = ProxyRemap.crosswire(b.skill, a)
    GameData.relink_card_backrefs(a); GameData.relink_card_backrefs(b)
    game.state.revision += 1  # implementer cache must rebuild
why_fun: A buried card's payoff wired to a spotlit card's trigger — topology surgery on the effect graph.
balance: Crossed wires stay crossed; untangling mid-show is not a service the crew offers.

name: The Lobbyist
historical_reference: The lobbyists who legalized Sunday shows, state by state
card_art: A smiling agent walking a law out of the courthouse under one arm
effect: Extract ONE rule card from the rules deck into your play deck: its rule stops applying, and it gains random pips (rule cards always carried them for exactly this day).
pseudocode:
# SEAM: the draft's own clause — rule cards have hidden random suit/rank 'in
# case player extracts them'. Extraction = rules-deck round trip + layout re-parse.
class SkillLobbyist extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var law : CardData = cue_pick_rules_deck()
    game.defer(func():
      game.move_rules_to_deck(law)      # Game deck fn: stage RULES -> DRAW
      law.suit = PipSuit.STANDARD[hash_pick(run_state().world_seed, 4)].new()
      GameData.relink_card_backrefs(law)
      game.reparse_board_layout())      # a stolen ZoneAdder = one column gone
why_fun: Stealing a LAW and shuffling it into your deck as a playable card — the Baba-Is-You promise, kept.
balance: Pocket the grab-legality law and enjoy anarchy; pocket a ZoneAdder and your board is four columns wide.

name: The Amendment
historical_reference: Sideshow banners repainted overnight — the show the sign promises changes by dawn
card_art: A quill signing a stamp onto a glowing law card, wax still soft
effect: Attach any stamp you own to a RULE card in the rules deck (Double Trigger on the Cascade Scorer runs the whole scoring pass twice...).
pseudocode:
# SEAM: rule cards are ordinary CardData — the stamp slot was always there.
# The stress: stamps now dispatch on ENGINE hooks (on_run_scorer et al.).
class SkillAmendment extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var law : CardData = cue_pick_rules_deck()
    law.stamp = cue_pick_owned_stamp().duplicate()
    GameData.relink_card_backrefs(law)
    game.state.revision += 1
    # engine rules opt OUT of U (combo_key '') — the stamp must inherit that
    # opt-out or a doubled scorer would register combo as a 'mod effect'
why_fun: Stamping the laws themselves — the modifier system eating its own tail, gloriously.
balance: Double Trigger on the wrong law doubles hazards as faithfully as scorers.

name: The Loophole
historical_reference: Barnum's contracts — read twice, they always said something else
card_art: A magnifying glass over a hazard's rule text, one clause glowing in reverse
effect: Choose one active town/boss hazard: its effect INVERTS to benefit you for this show (the snub pays double, the tax refunds, the hook feeds).
why_fun: Hazard towns become buff towns for the deck that reads the fine print — lawyering as an archetype.
balance: Inversion is literal: a hazard with no output inverts into nothing.

name: The Apprentice
historical_reference: The magician's apprentice — sworn to watch, forbidden to ask
card_art: A wide-eyed novice card, hands copying a gesture happening elsewhere
effect: Blank skill. Each show, it permanently LEARNS the first effect it observes firing (hook and behavior copied); holds up to three learned effects.
why_fun: A card whose final text is written by YOUR run — every copy in the world ends up different.
balance: It learns the FIRST thing it sees, including the mistake you fired by accident.

name: The Accountant
historical_reference: The red wagon's bookkeeper — the man who decided what the show had earned
card_art: A green eyeshade bent over two ledgers, points flowing from one to the other
effect: At payout (before R x C multiplies): shift up to 20% of the larger axis total onto the smaller. Costs 2 U to invoke (the books must balance somewhere).
pseudocode:
# SEAM: apply_act_score interposition — the payout formula grows a pre-hook.
# NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_apply_act_score(row_total, col_total) -> adjusted pair
class SkillAccountant extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_apply_act_score(r : BigNumber, c : BigNumber) -> Array[BigNumber]:
    if game.state.combo_classes.size() < 2: return [r, c]
    game.spend_combo(2)  # U is spendable (Exchange Rate precedent)
    return equalize_toward_mean(r, c, 0.2)  # balanced axes maximize R*C
why_fun: A lopsided board audited into a bigger product — multiplication finally hires staff.
balance: Two U per audit, and a board with one dead axis is beyond accounting.

name: The Groundhog
historical_reference: Groundhog Day (1993) — the day that repeats until you get it right
card_art: The same act performed twice, the second time with everyone slightly wiser
effect: Cue (once per show): replay the PREVIOUS act's entire effect sequence against the CURRENT board — same effects, same order, targets resolved fresh.
why_fun: Yesterday's perfect act, performed again on today's board — déjà vu as a finisher.
balance: The sequence is frozen; replayed onto the wrong board it is a beautiful irrelevance.

name: The Rewinder
historical_reference: The nickelodeon's hand-cranked rewinder — the past, sold by the foot
card_art: A film crank spooling glowing frames of earlier turns into a furnace
effect: Cue: burn your OLDEST undo snapshots — +points per snapshot destroyed (max 5 per act). Those moments become unreachable.
pseudocode:
# SEAM: save_history is data (Array[GameData], capped, persisted) — burning it
# stresses the undo contract AND the anti-cheat save (history ships with the run).
class SkillRewinder extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var n : int = min(5, game.save_history.size() - 1)  # never burn the present
    for i : int in n: game.save_history.pop_front()
    game.add_line_score(row_of(data), REWIND_PTS * n)
    RunManager.request_save()  # the shrunken history must persist (no resurrect)
why_fun: Selling your own safety net for points — regret, securitized.
balance: Every point is an undo you may desperately want three turns from now.

name: The Surveyor
historical_reference: The 24-hour man — chalk arrows on fence posts deciding where tomorrow lands
card_art: A surveyor's transit aimed down a column, chalk arrow pointing at the FLOOR
effect: While spotlit: YOUR drops resolve to the BOTTOM of the target column (ColumnStart) instead of on top — cards slide in beneath the stack.
why_fun: Placing UNDER stacks inverts every burial instinct in the game — foundations become a choice.
balance: Bottom-dropped cards are instantly covered: dark, safe, and silent.

name: The Ghost Writer
historical_reference: S.W. Erdnase — card handling's bible, author never identified
card_art: A pen writing on a blank card in a hand nobody recognizes
effect: Each show start: writes a temporary skill onto one blank/prop card — a copy of one effect that fired last show. The ink fades at show end.
why_fun: Your chaff wakes up each morning with yesterday's best line ghostwritten onto it.
balance: The ghost writes what FIRED, not what mattered; sometimes the ink is a honk.

name: The Dramaturg
historical_reference: The dramaturg — the theatre's structural editor, invisible and decisive
card_art: A red pen striking through a card's fine print, writing a longer clause
effect: Rewrite one card's combo identity: each of its triggers registers a FRESH combo key this act (it counts toward U every time, not just first activation).
why_fun: U-farming unlocked on one chosen engine — the multiplier curve bends where you point.
balance: This is the card the delta lever was built to punish; expect the errata.

name: The Time Signature
historical_reference: The Rite of Spring premiere, 1913 — the rhythm that started a riot
card_art: A conductor's score where the bar line falls between two pages
