effect: Splits the Submit: ROWS score at submit as normal; COLUMNS score at the START of the next turn, against whatever the board looks like then. U persists across the split.
why_fun: One turn to REARRANGE between the row pass and the column pass — the act becomes a two-move puzzle.
balance: Everything that assumed acts are atomic (undo, replay, banking) must now hold its breath with you.

name: The Fourth Wall
historical_reference: Pantomime's sacred contract — 'HE'S BEHIND YOU!' and the actor hears it
card_art: A card mid-scoring turning to face the audience, hand extended through the frame
effect: Once per act, DURING scoring resolution: the show pauses and you may make ONE grab/place before resolution continues (the audience shouts, and this time the act listens).
pseudocode:
# SEAM: B10 — no board mutation inside run_all_mods, live iteration. This card
# is the sanctioned exception: a SAFEPOINT between the row pass and col pass
# where the dispatch walk is NOT live. NEEDS NEW HOOK: resolution safepoint.
class SkillFourthWall extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_score_safepoint() -> void:  # fires between passes, outside the walk
    if game.state.wall_broken: return
    game.state.wall_broken = true
    await game.accept_one_player_move()  # full Board.move_stack, revision bump
    # the col pass now walks the MUTATED board — legal, because no walk is live
why_fun: Editing the board in the middle of its own scoring — the one move that makes spectators of every other card game.
balance: One move, mid-avalanche, on a clock: the audience shouts wrong sometimes.

name: The Marionette
historical_reference: Royal de Luxe — building-sized marionettes walked by teams of operators
card_art: Eleven strings descending from above the frame into one prop's shoulders
effect: Take direct control of one prop this act: it moves ONE slot per tick in the direction you steer (your inputs recorded into history — the replay steers identically).
why_fun: Flying the knife yourself, slot by slot — the prop layer's autonomy handed to your hands.
balance: A human-steered prop is exactly as accurate as the human; the mancala walk never missed.

name: The Convention
historical_reference: The game laws are called Conventions — and conventions can be amended
card_art: A gavel striking; a brand-new law card materializing in the hidden deck
effect: Enact ONE new law from a fixed slate of three (e.g. runs may wrap King-to-Ace; 4-card poker hands score; discards feed 10% of rank as points) — it becomes a rule card in the rules deck for the rest of the run.
pseudocode:
# SEAM: minting RULE cards at runtime — bounded templates, not freeform
# (the freeform rule-editor was vetoed as scope creep; a slate is the demoted form)
class SkillConvention extends CardModifierSkill:
  func on_cue() -> void:  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
    var law : CardData = CardData.new()
    law.skill = CONVENTION_SLATE[cue_pick(3)].new()  # var slate : Array[GDScript]
    law.suit = PipSuit.random_standard(); law.rank = hash_rank(run_state().world_seed, law)
    GameData.relink_card_backrefs(law)
    game.defer(func(): game.add_to_rules_deck(law))  # permanent for the run
why_fun: Writing law into the hidden deck yourself — the rule-deck architecture's promise, delivered inside its veto.
balance: A run-long law with no repeal clause; the wrong convention governs YOU hardest.

name: The Repertoire
historical_reference: Repertory companies — a different play every night, same troupe
card_art: One card fanning into three overlapping versions of itself, one lit at a time
effect: Holds THREE skills in one slot; the active one rotates each act (cue to rotate early). The other two sleep.
pseudocode:
# SEAM: CardData has ONE skill slot — multi-skill needs a container modifier
# that swaps the live skill in and out, with relink + cache invalidation each time.
class SkillRepertoire extends CardModifierSkill:
  var plays : Array[CardModifierSkill] = []  # size 3; holder keeps them alive
  func on_after_score() -> void: rotate()
  func on_cue() -> void: rotate()  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: on_cue
  func rotate() -> void:
    plays.push_back(data.skill); data.skill = plays.pop_front()
    GameData.relink_card_backrefs(data)
    game.state.revision += 1  # implementer cache must re-key
    game.register_combo(data.skill.combo_key(''))  # each face is a fresh U
why_fun: Three acts in one body, one deck slot — the modifier slot system bent double.
balance: Only one face is ever awake, and the rotation schedule waits for no meld.

name: The Ringmaster
historical_reference: The user's leader template — the master of ceremonies
card_art: A top-hatted figure mid-flourish, whip curled like a signature
effect: Leader: no suit or rank; starts the game in the Grand Entrance; always grabbable; placeable anywhere uncovered; REMAINS on board after submission. Optional flavor debuff: fear of fire/knives (cannot be placed on Flames/Knives cards).
why_fun: The one performer who never leaves the stage — continuity in a game of board wipes.
balance: No pips means no melds: he conducts, he never scores.

name: The Equestrian
historical_reference: Philip Astley, 1768 — the founder of the modern circus and its 42-foot ring
card_art: A rider standing on cantering horseback inside a chalked circle
effect: Leader (Ringmaster archetype): melds scored entirely within the First Ring (rows 1–5) get +mult — he standardized the ring for a reason.
why_fun: Rewards tight shallow boards — the founder's discipline as a build identity.
balance: Anti-synergy with every deep-ring and tall-column payoff in the catalog.

name: The Showman
historical_reference: P.T. Barnum — museums, hoaxes, and the Greatest Show on Earth
card_art: A beaming impresario gesturing at something that may not exist
effect: Leader (Producer): shops offer +1 extra card; all Gaff/Humbug/fake cards cost nothing to acquire.
pseudocode:
class SkillBarnum extends CardModifierSkill:  # + TypeLeader chassis
  # map/shop layer reads leader perks:
  func shop_extra_offers() -> int: return 1
  func price_scale(card : CardData) -> float:
    return 0.0 if is_gaff(card) else 1.0
why_fun: Free fakes forever — the Humbug economy gets its patron saint.
balance: A deck of free lies is one Prepared Town from bankruptcy.

name: Joey Grimaldi
historical_reference: Joseph Grimaldi — Regency London's superstar clown
card_art: A whiteface clown taking a pratfall that is secretly perfect geometry
effect: Leader (Clown): once per turn, a free Slapstick — swap the ranks of any two adjacent cards without spending a cue.
pseudocode:
class SkillGrimaldi extends CardModifierSkill:  # + TypeLeader chassis
  func on_next() -> void: game.state.free_slapstick = true
  # GameView: while free_slapstick, adjacent-pair rank swap costs nothing
why_fun: A free pip fix every single turn — the board never quite stays broken.
balance: One swap, adjacent only; he tidies, he does not transform.

name: The Escapologist
historical_reference: Harry Houdini — no lock held him, including the game's
card_art: A figure shrugging out of chains that are still deciding they are locked
effect: Leader (Escape Artist): your free undo becomes 2 per turn; all debuffs on your cards expire one show sooner.
why_fun: Permission to experiment squared — the take-back leader for players who think with their hands.
balance: He adds zero points; his whole kingdom is mistakes unmade.

name: The Tiger Queen
historical_reference: Mabel Stark — the great tiger trainer, working cats into her seventies
card_art: A small woman in white leathers dwarfed by, and dwarfing, five tigers
effect: Leader (Animal Trainer): consumption effects have NO cap (Hippo's 13 limit removed; Tigers/Gluttony rank gains unbounded).
pseudocode:
class SkillStark extends CardModifierSkill:  # + TypeLeader chassis
  func on_active() -> void: game.state.consumption_uncapped = true
  # eater skills consult state.consumption_uncapped before cap checks
why_fun: Uncapped appetite — the eating archetype's ceiling simply removed.
balance: The deck-devouring downside is also uncapped; she brakes for no one.

name: The Sibyl
historical_reference: The midway mitt-camp tradition — Madame Zora's lineage
card_art: A veiled reader behind a crystal ball in which tiny cards swim
effect: Leader (Fortune Teller): the top of the draw deck is always revealed; the first reroll at every shop is free.
why_fun: Permanent foresight plus shop grease — the information build's quiet monarch.
balance: All map and knowledge, no board presence whatsoever.

name: The Cannon King
historical_reference: Ildebrando Zacchini — the human-cannonball dynasty's patriarch
card_art: A mustachioed engineer patting an enormous cannon like a pet
effect: Leader (Special Effects): Fireworks-suit cards may score from the Grand Entrance (launched without ever entering the Ring).
why_fun: Scoring from the waiting room — the Entrance becomes an artillery battery.
balance: Only Fireworks, the suit you can barely acquire; the cannon is picky about ammunition.

name: The Sweet Tooth
historical_reference: William Morrison — the dentist who co-invented cotton candy
card_art: A kindly dentist holding a pink cloud bigger than his head
effect: Leader (Concessions): all +rank buffs your effects grant are PERMANENT instead of per-show.
pseudocode:
class SkillMorrison extends CardModifierSkill:  # + TypeLeader chassis
  func on_active() -> void: game.state.buffs_permanent = true
  # buff-granting skills consult state.buffs_permanent when applying
why_fun: Every sweetening compounds forever — the deck slowly candies itself into a monster.
balance: Slow exponential: early shows he is the weakest leader on the road.

name: The Costumier
historical_reference: The wardrobe mistresses who ran the great shows from behind a curtain
card_art: A pincushion-armed figure fitting a card for its second sash
effect: Leader (Costume Designer): all your cards may hold 2 stamps.
pseudocode:
class SkillCostumier extends CardModifierSkill:  # + TypeLeader chassis
  func on_active() -> void: game.state.stamp_capacity_bonus = 1
  # stamp application consults capacity bonus
why_fun: Doubles the entire stamp system's ceiling in one line of text.
balance: Twice the slots is twice the stamps you must actually FIND and pay for.

name: The Sharpshooter
historical_reference: Annie Oakley — shot holes through playing cards; free tickets still bear her name
card_art: A rifle resting across a saddle, a playing card downrange with five holes
effect: Leader (precision/formation): choose a rank at each show start; cards of that rank score DOUBLE this show.
why_fun: Calling your shot every show — deck construction becomes marksmanship.
balance: A called rank that never gets drawn is a blank round.

name: The Satirist
historical_reference: Vladimir Durov — exiled from cities for mocking officials via trained pigs
card_art: A dignified trainer beside a pig wearing a mayor's sash
effect: Leader (Animal Trainer/heel): shows earn +25% fame, but each town's SNUB/hazard is 1 tier harsher (satire has consequences).
pseudocode:
class SkillDurov extends CardModifierSkill:  # + TypeLeader chassis
  func on_active() -> void:
    run_state().fame_scale = 1.25
    run_state().hazard_tier_bonus = 1
why_fun: The heel run — being profitably hated is a whole second way to tour.
balance: You sharpened every knife the map will throw at you.

name: The Juggler King
historical_reference: Enrico Rastelli — the god of jugglers, dead at 34, records still argued
card_art: A juggler at the apex of ten simultaneous arcs, utterly calm
effect: Leader (Balls archetype): Balls-suit cards can be kept 'aloft' — re-scored each act — as long as at least one Balls card scores every submit.
why_fun: The juggle that must not stop — a streak mechanic living on your board.
balance: One act without a Balls score and every plate hits the floor at once.

name: The Conjuror
historical_reference: Shōkyokusai Tenkatsu — Meiji Japan's superstar magician, who ran her own troupe
card_art: A magician in a hakama producing a pigeon from a folded fan
effect: Leader (Magician): once per turn, produce a rank-1 token card of any suit into the Entrance (the troupe never runs out of doves).
why_fun: An endless gentle stream of bodies — glue, chaff, chorus, and prop-bait on demand.
balance: Rank-1 confetti dilutes every poker row it drifts into.

name: The Engineer
historical_reference: The Great Farini — Niagara crosser turned impresario who DESIGNED Zazel's cannon
card_art: A frock-coated figure at a drafting table covered in launch trajectories
effect: Leader (Special Effects): danger acts (gamble-tagged cards) cost 1 less to acquire and their destruction odds are halved (he builds the safety in).
pseudocode:
class SkillFarini extends CardModifierSkill:  # + TypeLeader chassis
  func price_scale(card : CardData) -> float:
    return 0.75 if card.has_tag(&'gamble') else 1.0
  func on_active() -> void: game.state.danger_odds_scale = 0.5
why_fun: Makes the whole daredevil catalog half as lethal — suddenly you CAN run three Bullet Catches.
balance: He mitigates variance, never adds power; a safe deck gains nothing.

name: The Aerialist
historical_reference: Lillian Leitzel — Ringling's biggest star; died in a rigging failure, 1931
card_art: A tiny figure spinning one-armed high above a dark ring, rosin dust falling
effect: Leader (Acrobat): re-scores the topmost meld once per submit (her endless planges). GLASS-FRAGILE: destroyed permanently if her column ever fails to score.
why_fun: The strongest leader in the game, one silent column from gone — the fragility IS the history, honored.
balance: Permanent death, mid-tour, by YOUR placement mistake; no other card asks this much care.

name: The Dalang
historical_reference: Wayang kulit — one person voicing every character all night long
card_art: A silhouetted puppeteer, dozens of card-shadows fanned in one raised hand
effect: Leader: while in play, YOU choose the trigger order of all card effects each scoring pass (dispatch order becomes player-controlled).
pseudocode:
class SkillDalang extends CardModifierSkill:  # + TypeLeader chassis
  func on_run_scorer() -> void:
    game.state.dispatch_order = player_order_ui(implementers())
  # run_all_mods consults state.dispatch_order instead of board order
  # NEEDS NEW HOOK: ordered dispatch override
why_fun: The player becomes the voice of everything — first-implementer-wins is finally YOURS.
balance: Total control means total responsibility: bad sequencing has no one left to blame.

name: The Panorama
historical_reference: Panoramas and cycloramas — the pre-cinema immersive picture halls
card_art: A folded paper strip opening into a painted horizon of the whole map
effect: Consumable (map): reveals the silhouette of the entire world map — every node's TYPE (show/booster/boss), none of its contents.
why_fun: One glance and the whole tour has a shape — planning becomes possible.
balance: Shapes, not contents: it tells you where, never what.

name: The Mitt Camp Reading
historical_reference: Midway palmistry booths — and a seeded RNG makes the prophecy HONEST
card_art: An upturned palm, one card pressed into it, the reader mid-sentence
effect: Consumable: pay 1 Gold; receive one TRUE fact about the next 3 nodes (seeded, so the prophecy cannot lie).
why_fun: Fortune-telling that is mechanically honest is funnier than fortune-telling that lies.
balance: One fact, chosen by the seed — true and useless are not exclusive.

name: The Raree Box
historical_reference: Peep shows — an itinerant's box of wonders, a coin a look
card_art: A wooden box with one brass eyehole, faint glow inside
effect: Consumable: pay 1 Gold; peek at the next 5 cards of the draw deck (order shown).
why_fun: A coin for certainty — the cheapest good decision on sale.
balance: Knowledge of a bad draw is still a bad draw.

name: Snake Oil
historical_reference: Clark Stanley, 'The Rattlesnake King' — the literal origin of the term
card_art: An ornate tonic bottle whose label promises everything
effect: Consumable: a cheap buff (+3 rank to one card this show) that is SOMETIMES (seeded 1-in-4) plain water — nothing happens.
why_fun: The variable-ratio buff — three doses work, and the fourth is the joke.
balance: A quarter of your money buys nothing, exactly as history promised.

name: The Showbag
historical_reference: Sideshow alley at the Australian Royal Shows — the showbag institution
card_art: A striped paper bag spilling commons, one wrapped sweet on top
effect: Consumable pack: 4 random Common cards, one guaranteed 'sweet' (a Concessions-class card).
why_fun: Cheap, cheerful, and someone always wants the sweet.
balance: Four commons is four commons; volume, not quality.

name: The Ouija Board
historical_reference: Patented 1891 — the parlor talking board that became an industry
card_art: A planchette resting on YES, nobody visibly touching it
effect: Consumable (3 charges): ask the deck one yes/no question per show ('Is card X in the top 10?', 'Does the next node's pack contain a stamp?') — answered truthfully from the seed.
why_fun: Interrogating your own deck through a haunted UI — information as a séance.
balance: Yes/no granularity: the spirits do not elaborate.

name: Spirit Photo
historical_reference: William Mumler's double-exposure ghosts — tried for fraud, beloved anyway
card_art: A tintype where a translucent second card hovers behind the sitter
effect: Consumable: capture a ghost copy of any card you own — the copy plays for ONE show (intangible, scores at half), then fades from the plate.
why_fun: Your best card, twice, for one night only — the double exposure is the fantasy.
balance: Half-strength and gone by morning; sentimentality is not a build.

name: The Patch
historical_reference: The circus 'patch' — the legal adjuster who smoothed things with officials
card_art: A rumpled man shaking hands with a sheriff, other hand behind his back
effect: Consumable: cancel ONE town penalty/hazard rule card before the show starts (it never enters the rules deck).
why_fun: Bureaucratic counterplay — you fix the town before the town happens.
balance: One hazard, pre-show, blind: you patch what you GUESS is coming.

name: Chindon'ya
historical_reference: Japan's costumed street bands hired to advertise shops — walking hype
card_art: A three-piece costumed band parading past a ticket booth
effect: Consumable: the NEXT town's fame requirement drops 15% (they warmed the crowd up before you arrived).
why_fun: Buying a softer landing — route planning gets a shock absorber.
balance: Fifteen percent of a goal you might have cleared anyway.

name: Hype Poster
historical_reference: The user's Hype mechanic — double the requirement, better the goodies
card_art: A poster promising THE IMPOSSIBLE in letters taller than the acts
effect: Consumable: voluntarily DOUBLE the next show's fame requirement; its rewards (pack quality, rarity odds) scale up two tiers.
why_fun: Self-selected difficulty with a poster's swagger — declare big, then be big.
balance: You wrote the check in ink; the deck still has to cash it.

name: The Advance Man
historical_reference: Billposters traveled weeks ahead of the show, marking the route
card_art: A man with a paste bucket disappearing up a road of fog
effect: Consumable: reveal all fog-of-war nodes within 2 edges of your position, including their contents.
why_fun: Two edges of certainty in a fogged tour — the scout earns his wage.
balance: Local knowledge only; the far road stays dark.

name: Paper the House
historical_reference: Giving away free tickets to look popular — 'papering' the house
card_art: A full grandstand where every third hand holds the same free ticket
effect: Consumable: auto-pass one town's fame check — but gain ZERO fame and zero rewards from it (the house was paper).
why_fun: The perfect skip button WITH a cost — dignity spent, tour preserved.
balance: A papered show pays nothing forward; the goal curve does not slow down with you.

name: The Rat Sheet
historical_reference: Circuses printed attack-posters slandering the rival ahead
card_art: A lurid poster denouncing a rival show, ink still venomous
effect: Consumable: raise the next Rival Show's difficulty for BOTH of you; if you both would pass, you also steal 25% of its fame payout.
why_fun: Mudslinging as an investment — you poisoned the well you both drink from.
balance: You raised your own wall too; the slander can beat you both.

name: The Cooler
historical_reference: The cold deck — swapping a stacked deck into the game
card_art: A second deck sliding from a sleeve, indistinguishable and perfect
effect: Consumable (once per run): replace your remaining draw deck with a perfectly ordered copy (you choose the order).
why_fun: Once per run you get to feel like a criminal — and the deck deals exactly your dream.
balance: One use, and a perfectly ordered deck still cannot fix a bad board.

name: The Claque
historical_reference: Paris opera's professional applauders — applause packages and blackmail
card_art: Six identical men in one row, clapping in perfect unison, eyes cold
effect: Consumable: guaranteed minimum fame this show (the floor rises to 60% of goal)... but the claque demands 1 Gold at EVERY subsequent show, forever.
why_fun: A protection racket you invited in — and the minute you stop paying, they boo.
balance: The subscription outlives the benefit by the whole rest of the tour.

name: Genie's Wish
historical_reference: The user's Genie scenario — make a wish
card_art: A brass lamp exhaling a card-shaped wish
effect: Consumable: choose any card in your deck; create a full copy of it in your hand (Entrance) for this show, permanent in deck after.
why_fun: Doubling your best card is the wish everyone makes first — and it is exactly as good as it sounds.
balance: Copy inflation: the second Bullet Catch also carries the second 1-in-13.

name: The Discoverie
historical_reference: Reginald Scot, 1584 — exposing conjuring to save accused witches; James I burned it
card_art: A heavy banned book open to a woodcut of the cups and balls
effect: Consumable (book, 3 readings): reveal every Gaff/fake in play (yours, the shop's, the town's). BANNED in pious towns — using it there is a crime event.
why_fun: Exposure as mercy — the book that saves you from every mermaid on the midway.
balance: Three readings, and in the wrong town the book costs more than the lie.

name: The Tower
historical_reference: The Major Arcana — trionfi, the trumps that predate the mystique
card_art: A lightning-struck tower of cards, two figures falling gracefully
effect: Consumable (Major Arcana): destroy an entire column — every card in it — and score half their summed base as flat points.
why_fun: The board's biggest single explosion — sometimes the stuck column just has to go.
balance: Your cards, your points, half price, gone forever.

name: The Wheel
historical_reference: The Major Arcana — Fortune's wheel
card_art: A carnival wheel whose slots are ranks, mid-spin
effect: Consumable (Major Arcana): re-roll the ranks of every card in one row (seeded).
why_fun: Spinning five fates at once — the whole row holds its breath with you.
balance: Fortune's wheel does not take requests.

name: The Hanged Man
historical_reference: The Major Arcana — the inverted man who sees differently
card_art: A stack of cards serenely upside down, halo at the bottom
effect: Consumable (Major Arcana): flip the ORDER of one entire stack (bottom becomes top).
why_fun: The buried engine surfaces, the dead weight sinks — one inversion, whole new column.
balance: Everything flips: the good card rises AND the shield you built above it drowns.

name: The Guest Star
historical_reference: Sitting Bull toured one season with the Wild West, on his own terms
card_art: A legendary silhouette stepping off a train, luggage carried by awe
effect: Consumable (event-granted): a random Legendary card joins your deck for exactly 3 shows, then departs — kept permanently if a hidden condition is met during its stay.
why_fun: Three shows with a legend — and maybe, if you are worthy, forever.
balance: Building around a card that is already packing its bags.

name: Golden Buzzer
historical_reference: The Golden Buzzer — one press, straight through, confetti
card_art: A huge gold button mid-slam, confetti frozen at the top of its arc
effect: Consumable (once per run): skip one town's requirement ENTIRELY — instant win, full rewards, confetti.
why_fun: Rare, unearned-feeling, delightful — exactly one free miracle per tour.
balance: Spent on a show you could have won, it is the most expensive confetti alive.

name: Deus ex Machina
historical_reference: The Greek crane god — criticized as a cheat for 2,400 years, still hired
card_art: A golden crane arm lowering a serene figure into a collapsing finale
effect: Consumable (once per run): when a show is LOST, it is instead won at exactly goal (the god descends; the plot resolves). Deliberately feels like cheating.
pseudocode:
class ConsumableDeusEx extends BoosterTemplate:
  func use() -> void:  # usable at the loss screen
    game.state.total = game.state.goal
    game.override_outcome(WIN)
why_fun: The most honest name in the catalog — a cheat, labeled as a cheat, priced as a cheat.
balance: It saves one night, teaches nothing, and the next wall is taller.

name: The Major Arcana
historical_reference: The trionfi — trumps were a GAME for 300 years before the mystics arrived; Pamela Colman Smith drew the deck everyone knows and died uncredited
card_art: Twenty-two cards fanned in an arc, each glowing a different discipline
effect: Consumable SERIES: 22 unique one-shot consumables (Tower/Wheel/Hanged Man ship first; the rest follow) — collect all 22 across runs for a profile reward honoring the Uncredited Artist.
why_fun: Long-term chase content with a real ghost at the end — the artist finally credited.
balance: A collection spanning many runs; no single copy wins you anything tonight.

name: The Designated Loser
historical_reference: The Washington Generals — 13,000 losses; their 1971 win made children cry
card_art: A cheerful opposing troupe already rehearsing their bow
effect: Town hazard (morale node): a 'boss' show with a trivial goal you always beat... with a hidden 1-in-1000 (seeded) chance the Generals WIN — the goal silently triples mid-show.
why_fun: A guaranteed win that is not QUITE guaranteed — and the day it happens, the story is legendary.
balance: The one-in-a-thousand run that loses a freebie will not find it funny.

name: Lot Lice
historical_reference: 'Lot lice' — townsfolk who watched the setup for free
card_art: A row of spectators leaning on the fence, wallets conspicuously closed
effect: Town hazard: your FIRST submit each show scores 0 fame (they already watched for free).
why_fun: Compresses the show into two real acts — pacing pressure with a slang lesson.
balance: Effectively lose a third of the show; front-loaded decks suffer most.

name: Mud Show
historical_reference: 'Mud show' — small circuses slogging unpaved roads between towns
card_art: Wagon wheels sunk to the axle, performers pushing in costume
effect: Town hazard: movement tax — every stack pickup this show costs 1 discard from the deck top.
pseudocode:
class HazardMudShow extends CardModifierSkill:  # temp rules_deck card
  func on_can_grab_stack(stack) -> Variant:
    game.defer(func(): game.discard_from_deck_top())  # the toll
    return null  # legality unchanged; the mud just takes its cut
why_fun: Every rearrangement has a price tag — suddenly you play like a chess master.
balance: Fidgety players bleed their deck into the mud.

name: Knifetown's Snub
historical_reference: The user's Knifetown — unimpressed by knife scoring
card_art: A town square of crossed arms, every local wearing a knife
effect: Town hazard: Knives-suit effects score nothing here (they have seen knives).
pseudocode:
class HazardKnifetown extends CardModifierSkill:  # temp rules_deck card
  func on_prop_passing(prop) -> Variant:
    if prop.kind == KNIFE: return NEGATE_PASS  # knives fly, pay zero
    return null
why_fun: The boss-blind resilience test, suit by suit — hyperfocus meets its bill.
balance: A knife-heavy deck plays this town half-blind.

name: Hooptown's Snub
historical_reference: The user's Hooptown
card_art: A town where every doorway, window, and dog collar is already a hoop
effect: Town hazard: Hoops-suit effects score nothing here.
pseudocode:
class HazardHooptown extends CardModifierSkill:  # temp rules_deck card
  func on_prop_passing(prop) -> Variant:
    if prop.kind == HOOP: return NEGATE_PASS
    return null
why_fun: Same test, different church — the map keeps every archetype honest.
balance: Talent-sweep builds lose their engine at the gate.

name: Juggletown's Snub
historical_reference: The user's Juggletown
card_art: A juggling town — even the babies keep three rattles aloft
effect: Town hazard: Balls-suit effects score nothing here (Juggling statuses still apply, but pay 0).
pseudocode:
class HazardJuggletown extends CardModifierSkill:  # temp rules_deck card
  func on_prop_passing(prop) -> Variant:
    if prop.kind == BALL: return NEGATE_PASS
    return null
why_fun: The setup suit gets snubbed — your deferred-value engine idles for one town.
balance: Juggling seeded here pays out only if you survive to the NEXT town.

name: Firetown's Snub
historical_reference: The user's Firetown
card_art: A town of smiths and chimneys, politely bored by flame
effect: Town hazard: Flames-suit effects score nothing here; Burning statuses do not multiply.
pseudocode:
class HazardFiretown extends CardModifierSkill:  # temp rules_deck card
  func on_prop_passing(prop) -> Variant:
    if prop.kind == FIRE: return NEGATE_PASS
    return null
why_fun: The cascade build's cold shower — one town where fire is just warm.
balance: Burning stacks built here are pure wasted setup.

name: Temperance Town
historical_reference: Dry towns on the historical circus routes
card_art: A banner across Main Street: NO SPIRITS, NO SWEETS, NO NONSENSE
effect: Town hazard: Concessions-class cards disabled; shop prices doubled here.
pseudocode:
class HazardTemperance extends CardModifierSkill:  # temp rules_deck card
  func on_active() -> void:
    game.state.disabled_class = &'Concessions'
    run_state().shop_price_scale = 2.0
why_fun: A class ban that makes route planning matter — the sweet decks detour.
balance: Concessions decks lose healers AND face gouged prices in one stop.

name: The Fire Marshal
historical_reference: Post-1944 Hartford fire regulations reshaped tenting forever
card_art: A stern inspector holding a clipboard between a torch and a tent
effect: Town hazard: Flames-suit cards cannot score; any Flames card in your DECK is treated as Wax type this show.
why_fun: Regulation as a boss — the fire deck meets the one authority it cannot burn.
balance: Harsher than a snub: your fire cards actively melt here.

name: John Robinson
historical_reference: 'John Robinson' — the backstage code for CUT THE SHOW SHORT
card_art: A stage manager drawing a finger across his throat, gently
effect: Elite hazard: shortened show — 2 submits instead of 3 (the goal does not shrink).
why_fun: One act stolen — every setup line in your deck gets audited for speed.
balance: Slow-build engines (Hunger Artist, Fireworks ramps) simply do not fit in two acts.

name: The Hook
historical_reference: Amateur night's shepherd's crook — bad acts yanked mid-bow
card_art: A long crook emerging from stage left, already curled around an ankle
effect: Town hazard: after each submit, your lowest-ranked board card is yanked (discarded) unless it scored that act.
why_fun: Every act must earn its slot — the crook keeps the whole roster honest.
balance: Glue cards (Blanks, tokens) get eaten exactly because they never score.

name: Blowdown
historical_reference: Tent blowdowns — the traveling show's most feared weather
card_art: Canvas mid-collapse, ropes snapping, one lantern still lit
effect: Weather boss: at show start and each Next, a random (seeded) Grand Entrance card is discarded. The show must go on.
why_fun: The storm forces triage every single turn — protective stamps finally headline.
balance: Random loss punishes thin decks brutally; the Vine and Digital become gospel.

name: Hey Rube!
historical_reference: 'Hey Rube!' — the rallying cry when the circus fought the town
card_art: A brawl boiling at the tent flap, one performer mid-haymaker
effect: Boss: each submit, the rightmost column is ATTACKED — every card there gets Exhausted unless a Stuntsman/Strongman (Escape Artist class) stands in it.
why_fun: A geographic threat with a garrison mechanic — you post guards like a real lot boss.
balance: One column is conscripted all show; your Escape Artists become furniture.

name: Railroad Jump
historical_reference: The brutal overnight railroad jumps of the golden age
card_art: A train receding into dark, one card left standing on the platform
effect: Route hazard (map edge): traveling this long edge costs one card from your deck — left on the platform (removed for the run).
why_fun: The route itself takes a toll — geography becomes a body count.
balance: Forced attrition; the long shortcut is never free.

name: The Volcano
historical_reference: The user's volcano note — performing near lava has consequences
card_art: A distant crater glow; the board's bottom row shimmering with heat
effect: Terrain boss: the bottom row of the Ring is LAVA — any card ending a turn there takes Burning and loses 1 rank per turn.
why_fun: The floor is literally lava — every column plans its own escape ladder.
balance: Deep columns must build UP fast or cook from below.

name: The Gong
historical_reference: The Gong Show — failure as the entertainment
card_art: An enormous brass gong, mallet already in motion, crowd delighted
effect: Town hazard: any submit scoring under a floor (25% of goal) ends the show IMMEDIATELY — the crowd loves the gong more than the acts.
why_fun: No sandbagging, no warm-up acts — every submit must clear the bar or the mallet falls.
balance: Setup acts (banking, seeding statuses) are exactly what gets gonged.

name: The Prepared Town
historical_reference: Johnny Carson, ex-magician, controlled the props himself — nothing bent for Geller that night
card_art: A town square where every surface has been dusted for fingerprints
effect: Boss: pre-inspects everything — all Gaff/Humbug/fake/Masked cards are revealed and disabled for this show.
why_fun: The anti-fake audit every bluff deck fears — the town that did its homework.
balance: An honest deck walks through untouched; a Humbug deck arrives unemployed.

name: The Loggione
historical_reference: La Scala's gallery — connoisseurs who have booed the world's best off stage
card_art: A high balcony of opera glasses, all trained, none impressed
effect: Town hazard: Common cards score NOTHING here; Legendary cards score TRIPLE (the connoisseurs only rise for greatness).
why_fun: The anti-Kecak town — for one night, your deck's ceiling matters more than its floor.
balance: Commons-swarm decks meet a wall of folded arms.

name: Hall of Mirrors
historical_reference: The funhouse — distortion as delight
card_art: A boss board reflected wrong: familiar cards with unfamiliar smiles
effect: Boss: the show duplicates your board's reflection with FAKE copies of your cards (Gaffs) interleaved into rows — they score 0 and you must play around them.
why_fun: Fighting funhouse copies of your own deck — the boss is literally you, warped.
balance: Board space chokes fast; every meld must dodge your own reflections.

name: The Falling Wall
historical_reference: Buster Keaton, Steamboat Bill Jr. — the façade falls, the window saves him
card_art: A house wall mid-fall, one open window aligned with one calm card
effect: Boss attack (telegraphed one turn ahead): destroys an ENTIRE column except one exact row slot — stand your card in the window.
why_fun: The purest spatial-skill moment available — one turn to find the window, then the wall speaks.
balance: One column will be rubble; the question is only what stands in the frame.

name: The Rival Show
historical_reference: Circus wars — Barnum vs. Forepaugh, Ringling vs. everyone
card_art: Two big tops glaring at each other across one small town
effect: Recurring boss: a rival plays your town first — the goal arrives pre-raised 30%; BEAT it and steal one card from the rival's themed deck.
why_fun: Losing to them stings; ROBBING them afterwards is the whole revenge fantasy.
balance: The pre-raised goal lands whether or not your deck was ready for a war.

name: The Anarchist
historical_reference: Rejected as a player card (silencing the rule deck breaks everything) — reborn as the boss it always was
card_art: A grinning figure holding a lit match beside the rules deck
effect: Boss: each submit, ONE of your beneficial rule cards (Free Undo, Soundcheck, your added rules) is disabled for the rest of the show, in the order the Anarchist prefers.
why_fun: Your infrastructure crumbling one law at a time — the rule deck finally under siege.
balance: By act three the game you built is not the game you are playing.

name: The Marathon
historical_reference: Depression-era dance marathons — endurance ground into spectacle
card_art: Exhausted couples still swaying under a WEEK 3 banner
effect: Boss gauntlet: a 3-show run where the board does NOT fully reset between shows (cards, statuses, and lava stay) and goals stack cumulatively.
why_fun: Consequences that survive the night — a three-show story your board tells in scars.
balance: Every mistake compounds for three shows; there is no clean morning.

name: The Balance
historical_reference: Barong vs. Rangda, Bali — the battle that neither side ever wins
card_art: A lion-spirit and a witch mid-eternal-clash, scales hanging level between them
effect: Final-tier boss: CANNOT be beaten by score — win by ending the show with row total and column total within 10% of each other (balance, not victory).
why_fun: The alt-win-con the design notes kept searching for — a boss that judges harmony, not size.
balance: Every number-go-up instinct you trained all run is suddenly the enemy.
